GenAI,How do I compare strings in Java?,"

I've been using the == operator in my program to compare all my strings so far.
However, I ran into a bug, changed one of them into .equals() instead, and it fixed the bug.
Is == bad? When should it and should it not be used? What's the difference?

,

== tests for reference equality (whether they are the same object).
.equals() tests for value equality (whether they contain the same data).
Objects.equals() checks for null before calling .equals() so you don't have to (available as of JDK7, also available in Guava).
Consequently, if you want to test whether two strings have the same value you will probably want to use Objects.equals().
<pre><code>// These two have the same value
// ... but they are not the same object
// ... but these are because literals are interned by
// the compiler and thus refer to the same object
// ... string literals are concatenated by the compiler
// ... but you should really just call Objects.equals()
From the Java Language Specification JLS 15.21.3. Reference Equality Operators == and !=:

While == may be used to compare references of type String, such an equality test determines whether or not the two operands refer to the same String object. The result is false if the operands are distinct String objects, even if they contain the same sequence of characters (§3.10.5, §3.10.6). The contents of two strings s and t can be tested for equality by the method invocation s.equals(t).
You almost always want to use Objects.equals(). In the rare situation where you know you're dealing with interned strings, you can use ==.

Moreover, a string literal always refers to the same instance of class String. This is because string literals - or, more generally, strings that are the values of constant expressions (§15.28) - are "

column1: Similar examples can also be found in JLS 3.10.5-1.
String.equalsIgnoreCase() value equality that ignores case. Beware, however, that this method can have unexpected results in various locale-related cases, see this question.

String.contentEquals() compares the content of the String with the content of any CharSequence (available since Java 1.5). Saves you from having to turn your StringBuffer, etc into a String before doing the equality comparison, but leaves the null checking to you.

, In Java, comparing strings using the `==` operator checks if both references point to the same object in memory, not if their values are equal. This is different from comparing strings using the `.equals()` method, which checks if the values of two strings are identical.
Let me provide an example to illustrate the difference:
if (str1 == str2) { // this will evaluate to false
System.out.println(""str1 and str2 are the same object"")
if (str1.equals(str2)) { // this will evaluate to true
System.out.println(""str1 and str2 have the same value"")

In the example above, `str1` and `str2` have the same value, but they are not the same object, so the comparison using the `==` operator will not yield the expected result. However, when we use the `.equals()` method, the comparison will correctly return `true`.

A typical Java IDE has support for running Java applications in the IDE JVM itself or in a child JVM. These are generally immune from this particular exception, because the IDE uses its own mechanisms to construct the runtime classpath, identify the main class and create the java command line.

However it is still possible for this exception to occur, if you do things behind the back of the IDE. For example, if you have previously set up an Application Launcher for your Java app in Eclipse, and you then moved the JAR file containing the "
column3: class to a different place in the file system without telling Eclipse, Eclipse would unwittingly launch the JVM with an incorrect classpath.

column1: In short, if you get this problem in an IDE, check for things like stale IDE state, broken project references or broken launcher configurations.

It is also possible for an IDE to simply get confused. IDE's are hugely complicated pieces of software comprising many interacting parts. Many of these parts adopt various caching strategies in order to make the IDE as a whole responsive. These can sometimes go wrong, and one possible symptom is problems when launching applications. If you suspect this could be happening, it is worth trying other things like restarting your IDE, rebuilding the project and so on.
• From the Oracle Java Tutorials - Common Problems (and Their Solutions)

, The error message ""Could not find or load main class"" indicates that the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) cannot find the main class that is specified in the command-line arguments or in the manifest file of a JAR file. The main class is the entry point of a Java application and is the class that contains the `main` method, which is executed when the application is started.
There are several reasons why this error may occur:

1. Incorrect class name: Make sure that the class name is spelled correctly and matches the name of the file. The file name and the class name should have the same name, except for the extension. For example, if the class is named `MyClass`, the file name should be `MyClass.java`.

2. Class not in the classpath: The JVM searches for the main class in the classpath. The classpath specifies the location of the Java classes and resources that your application uses. If the main class is not in the classpath, the JVM cannot find it. You can add the classpath to the command-line arguments or to the build file (such as `build.gradle` or `pom.xml`) of your project.

What are Null Pointer Exceptions (java.lang.NullPointerException) and what causes them?
What methods/tools can be used to determine the cause so that you stop the exception from causing the program to terminate prematurely?

There are two overarching types of variables in Java:

• Primitives: variables that contain data. If you want to manipulate the data in a primitive variable you can manipulate that variable directly. By convention primitive types start with a lowercase letter. For example variables of type int or char are primitives.

• References: variables that contain the memory address of an Object i.e. variables that refer to an Object. If you want to manipulate the Object that a reference variable refers to you must dereference it. Dereferencing usually entails using . to access a method or field, or using [ to index an array. By convention reference types are usually denoted with a type that starts in uppercase. For example variables of type Object are references.
Consider the following code where you declare a variable of primitive type int and don't initialize it:

List of situations that cause a NullPointerException to occur
Here are all the situations in which a NullPointerException occurs, that are directly* mentioned by the Java Language Specification:
• Accessing (i.e. getting or setting) an instance field of a null reference. (static fields don't count!)
• Calling an instance method of a null reference. (static methods don't count!)
• Any integer/floating point operator can throw a NullPointerException if one of its operands is a boxed null reference
• An unboxing conversion throws a NullPointerException if the boxed value is null.
• Calling super on a null reference throws a NullPointerException. If you are confused, this is talking about qualified superclass constructor invocations:

Regarding the ordering of elements, Java Map does not maintain a specific order for its entries. However, some specific Map implementations, like `LinkedHashMap` and `HashMap` with `HashMap.Entry.accessOrder()` set as a value when creating a new instance, do maintain an order (linked or insertion order). So, if you need to iterate over the map entries in a specific order, you should consider using one of these implementations.

It's important to note that the ordering provided by these implementations is not guaranteed to remain constant across different JVM invocations. , In Java, access modifiers determine the visibility and accessibility of class members, including fields, methods, and nested classes. Here's a detailed explanation of each access modifier and when to use them in Java:

A member declared as `public` can be accessed from any part of the program, including other classes and packages. It is the most permissive access modifier. For example, a public class can be imported and used in another project, and a public method can be called from any other class or object.

A member without any explicit access modifier is package-private, meaning it can only be accessed within the same package. It is useful when you want to hide implementation details from outside the package but still allow other classes within the package to use it. For instance, in a utility class within a package, methods without access modifiers can be used by other classes within the same package.

What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
It depends on what form of date / time you want:

• If you want the date / time as a single numeric value, then System.currentTimeMillis() gives you that, expressed as the number of milliseconds after the UNIX epoch (as a Java long). This value is a delta from a UTC time-point, and is independent of the local time-zone1.
• If you want the date / time in a form that allows you to access the components (year, month, etc) numerically, you could use one of the following:
• new Date() gives you a Date object initialized with the current date / time. The problem is that the Date API methods are mostly flawed ... and deprecated.

• Calendar.getInstance() gives you a Calendar object initialized with the current date / time, using the default Locale and TimeZone. Other overloads allow you to use a specific Locale and/or TimeZone. Calendar works ... but the APIs are still cumbersome.

• new org.joda.time.DateTime() gives you a Joda-time object initialized with the current date / time, using the default time zone and chronology. There are lots of other Joda alternatives ... too many to describe here. (But note that some people report that Joda time has performance issues.
• in Java 8, calling java.time.LocalDateTime.now() and java.time.ZonedDateTime.now() will give you representations2 for the current date / time.
Prior to Java 8, most people who know about these things recommended Joda-time as having (by far) the best Java APIs for doing things involving time point and duration calculations.
With Java 8 and later, the standard java.time package is recommended. Joda time is now considered "

// both conditions a and b must be true

RAG,How do I compare strings in Java?,"

I've been using the == operator in my program to compare all my strings so far.
However, I ran into a bug, changed one of them into .equals() instead, and it fixed the bug.
Is == bad? When should it and should it not be used? What's the difference?

,

== tests for reference equality (whether they are the same object).
.equals() tests for value equality (whether they contain the same data).
Objects.equals() checks for null before calling .equals() so you don't have to (available as of JDK7, also available in Guava).
Consequently, if you want to test whether two strings have the same value you will probably want to use Objects.equals().
<pre><code>// These two have the same value
// ... but they are not the same object
// ... but these are because literals are interned by
// the compiler and thus refer to the same object
// ... string literals are concatenated by the compiler
// ... but you should really just call Objects.equals()
From the Java Language Specification JLS 15.21.3. Reference Equality Operators == and !=:

While == may be used to compare references of type String, such an equality test determines whether or not the two operands refer to the same String object. The result is false if the operands are distinct String objects, even if they contain the same sequence of characters (§3.10.5, §3.10.6). The contents of two strings s and t can be tested for equality by the method invocation s.equals(t).
You almost always want to use Objects.equals(). In the rare situation where you know you're dealing with interned strings, you can use ==.

Moreover, a string literal always refers to the same instance of class String. This is because string literals - or, more generally, strings that are the values of constant expressions (§15.28) - are "
column3: so as to share unique instances, using the method String.intern.

column1: Similar examples can also be found in JLS 3.10.5-1.
String.equalsIgnoreCase() value equality that ignores case. Beware, however, that this method can have unexpected results in various locale-related cases, see this question.

String.contentEquals() compares the content of the String with the content of any CharSequence (available since Java 1.5). Saves you from having to turn your StringBuffer, etc into a String before doing the equality comparison, but leaves the null checking to you.

, To compare strings in Java, it is not recommended to use the `==` operator as strings are objects and each string variable holds a reference to a string object in memory. Instead, use the `equals()` method, which tests whether two strings contain the same characters. For instance, the expression `greeting.equals(""Hello"")` checks if the string `greeting` contains the same sequence of characters as the string literal `""Hello""`. (p. 214)

Using the `==` operator for string comparison may lead to unexpected results as it tests whether the two string references point to the same object in memory. For example, if you have two string variables `str1` and `str2` that refer to different string objects with the same content, `str1 == str2` will be false even though their contents are equal.

The `compareTo()` method can be used for comparing strings lexicographically (based on their Unicode values). For example, `str1.compareTo(str2)` returns a negative number if `str1` comes before `str2` alphabetically, zero if they are the same, and a positive number if `str1` comes after `str2`. (p. 380)

In summary, to compare strings in Java, use the `equals()` method for testing string contents and the `compareTo()` method for comparing strings lexicographically. Avoid using the `==` operator for string comparison as it tests for reference equality. , The error message ""Could not find or load main class"" indicates that the Java interpreter is unable to locate or execute the main class of a Java program. This could be due to several reasons:

1. Incorrect class path: The Java interpreter searches for class files based on a list called the class path. Class files must be located in one of the directories or jar files specified in the class path. If the main class file is not in a directory or jar file included in the class path, the interpreter will not be able to find it.
2. Incorrect main class name: The name of the main class must match the name of the Java file. For example, if the main class is named ""HelloWorld"", the Java file must be named ""HelloWorld.java"".

3. Missing or incorrect main method: The main method must be defined in the main class with the correct signature (public static void main(String[] args)). The main method is the entry point for the Java program and determines how and in what order other methods are executed.
To resolve the issue, you can try the following steps:

1. Check that the class path includes the location of the main class file. You can modify the class path by adding the directory or jar file containing the main class to the command used to run the Java interpreter. For example, if your main class is in a directory called ""myclasses"" located in the current directory, you can run the Java interpreter with the command ""java -cp .:myclasses MyMainClass"".
2. Ensure that the main class name matches the name of the Java file.
3. Verify that the main method is defined correctly in the main class.

If you are using a JavaFX program, you may also need to include the JavaFX SDK on the class path if your program requires it. You can do this by adding the directory containing the JavaFX SDK to the class path.

Additionally, if you are creating an executable jar file, you must specify which class contains the main method when running the jar file. You can do this by adding the -e option to the command, followed by the full name of the class. For example, ""java -jar MyProgram.jar -e com.mypackage.MyMainClass"".

RAG,"What is a NullPointerException, and how do I fix it?","

What are Null Pointer Exceptions (java.lang.NullPointerException) and what causes them?
What methods/tools can be used to determine the cause so that you stop the exception from causing the program to terminate prematurely?

,

There are two overarching types of variables in Java:

• Primitives: variables that contain data. If you want to manipulate the data in a primitive variable you can manipulate that variable directly. By convention primitive types start with a lowercase letter. For example variables of type int or char are primitives.

• References: variables that contain the memory address of an Object i.e. variables that refer to an Object. If you want to manipulate the Object that a reference variable refers to you must dereference it. Dereferencing usually entails using . to access a method or field, or using [ to index an array. By convention reference types are usually denoted with a type that starts in uppercase. For example variables of type Object are references.
Consider the following code where you declare a variable of primitive type int and don't initialize it:

These two lines will crash the program because no value is specified for x and we are trying to use x's value to specify y. All primitives have to be initialized to a usable value before they are manipulated.
Now here is where things get interesting. Reference variables can be set to null which means "
column3: . You can get a null value in a reference variable if you explicitly set it that way, or a reference variable is uninitialized and the compiler does not catch it (Java will automatically set the variable to null).

column1: If a reference variable is set to null either explicitly by you or through Java automatically, and you attempt to dereference it you get a NullPointerException.

The NullPointerException (NPE) typically occurs when you declare a variable but did not create an object and assign it to the variable before trying to use the contents of the variable. So you have a reference to something that does not actually exist.
The first line declares a variable named num, but it does not actually contain a reference value yet. Since you have not yet said what to point to, Java sets it to null.
In the second line, the new keyword is used to instantiate (or create) an object of type Integer, and the reference variable num is assigned to that Integer object.
If you attempt to dereference num before creating the object you get a NullPointerException. In the most trivial cases, the compiler will catch the problem and let you know that "

// Do something to obj, assumes obj is not null
In which case, you are not creating the object obj, but rather assuming that it was created before the doSomething() method was called. Note, it is possible to call the method like this:
In which case, obj is null, and the statement obj.myMethod() will throw a NullPointerException.

If the method is intended to do something to the passed-in object as the above method does, it is appropriate to throw the NullPointerException because it's a programmer error and the programmer will need that information for debugging purposes.

In addition to NullPointerExceptions thrown as a result of the method's logic, you can also check the method arguments for null values and throw NPEs explicitly by adding something like the following near the beginning of a method:

Note that it's helpful to say in your error message clearly which object cannot be null. The advantage of validating this is that 1) you can return your own clearer error messages and 2) for the rest of the method you know that unless obj is reassigned, it is not null and can be dereferenced safely.

Alternatively, there may be cases where the purpose of the method is not solely to operate on the passed in object, and therefore a null parameter may be acceptable. In this case, you would need to check for a null parameter and behave differently. You should also explain this in the documentation. For example, doSomething() could be written as:

List of situations that cause a NullPointerException to occur
Here are all the situations in which a NullPointerException occurs, that are directly* mentioned by the Java Language Specification:
• Accessing (i.e. getting or setting) an instance field of a null reference. (static fields don't count!)
• Calling an instance method of a null reference. (static methods don't count!)
• Any integer/floating point operator can throw a NullPointerException if one of its operands is a boxed null reference
• An unboxing conversion throws a NullPointerException if the boxed value is null.
• Calling super on a null reference throws a NullPointerException. If you are confused, this is talking about qualified superclass constructor invocations:

• foo.new SomeInnerClass() throws a NullPointerException when foo is null.
• Method references of the form name1::name2 or primaryExpression::name throws a NullPointerException when evaluated when name1 or primaryExpression evaluates to null.
a note from the JLS here says that, someInstance.someStaticMethod() doesn't throw an NPE, because someStaticMethod is static, but someInstance::someStaticMethod still throw an NPE!

, A NullPointerException is an error in Java that occurs when a program tries to use a variable that holds a null reference as if it were an object with instance variables and methods. This results in the program attempting to access memory locations that do not exist, leading to an exception being thrown.

To prevent NullPointerExceptions, it's essential to ensure that variables are initialized with non-null values before using them to access instance variables or invoke methods. For instance, before accessing an instance variable or invoking a method on an object, it's recommended to check if the variable holds a non-null value using the 'if (variable != null)' condition.
When a NullPointerException does occur, it can be challenging to determine the cause. To help diagnose the problem, several methods and tools can be employed:

1. Adding a try-catch block: In the code, you can utilize a try-catch block to handle NullPointerExceptions, which allows the program to gracefully recover from the exception instead of terminating abruptly.

2. Using a debugger: Employing a debugger, such as Eclipse's Debugging perspective, can help identify where the NullPointerException is occurring by stepping through the code line by line and examining variable values.

3. Printing stack traces: When a NullPointerException occurs, the Java Virtual Machine generates a stack trace, which contains a detailed list of the method calls leading up to the exception. This information can be helpful in determining the source of the problem.
4. Using assertions: Assertions can be employed to validate that variables hold expected values, helping to ensure that the program never attempts to use a null value.
Here's an example of using a try-catch block to handle a NullPointerException:

By following these methods and being aware of best practices, you can minimize the occurrence of NullPointerExceptions in your Java programs.",310,,,,,0.0,0.6219765631503512,0,[],[],141,1451,393,0,[],java,mistral:instruct

If the map is a `TreeMap`, the keys are visited in ascending order when using an iterator or a for-each loop. For a `HashMap`, the keys are visited in an arbitrary, unpredictable order.

You can also iterate through the values directly by using the `values()` method, which returns a `Collection<V>` containing all the values in the map. However, keep in mind that a `Collection` can contain duplicate elements, unlike a `Set`.

Regarding your second question, the ordering of elements in a Java Map depends on the specific map implementation that you have for the interface. When dealing with collections like ArrayLists, the 'for each' loop has the same syntax:

RAG,"What is the difference between public, protected, package-private and private in Java?","

In Java, are there clear rules on when to use each of access modifiers, namely the default (package private), public, protected and private, while making class and interface and dealing with inheritance?

,

The official tutorial may be of some use to you.

, In Java, the access modifiers `public`, `protected`, `package-private`, and `private` determine the scope or visibility of classes, interfaces, methods, and variables. Here's a detailed explanation of each access modifier, based on the provided text chunks from the ""Introduction to Programming Using Java"" textbook:

1. **Public**: When an element is declared as `public`, it can be accessed from any part of the program, regardless of the package it belongs to. This means that public classes, methods, and variables can be accessed from any other class in the program. For example, a public method in a class can be called from any other class, and a public variable in a class can be accessed directly from any other class.

2. **Protected**: Protected access modifier is used when dealing with inheritance. When a method or member variable is marked as protected, it can be accessed within the package and by the subclasses of the class, whether they are in the same package or not. For instance, if a class `Parent` has a protected variable, then a subclass `Child` can access it, but classes outside of the package cannot access it.

3. **Package-private (default)**: When no access modifier is specified, the element is considered package-private, meaning it can only be accessed within the package it belongs to. This is the default access level for elements in Java. For example, if a class is not explicitly placed in a package, it is part of the default package, and its elements can be accessed by other classes within the default package.

4. **Private**: A private element is accessible only within the enclosing class, and it cannot be accessed from outside the class. For instance, a private method or variable can only be used within the class where it is defined.

With the introduction of modules in Java, a module is a collection of packages, providing yet another level of containment. Modules contain packages which contain classes that have private, protected, and public members. Packages do not have to be in a module to be used, but all standard classes in Java and JavaFX have been divided into a set of packages. Modules were introduced to provide better access control. For more information on modules, refer to Sections 4.6.4 and 4.6.5 in the textbook.

It's also worth noting that it is considered good practice to make member variables and subroutines private, unless there is a specific reason to make them public or protected. This is to help maintain encapsulation and prevent unintended access to the internal workings of the class.

What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
It depends on what form of date / time you want:

• If you want the date / time as a single numeric value, then System.currentTimeMillis() gives you that, expressed as the number of milliseconds after the UNIX epoch (as a Java long). This value is a delta from a UTC time-point, and is independent of the local time-zone1.
• If you want the date / time in a form that allows you to access the components (year, month, etc) numerically, you could use one of the following:
• new Date() gives you a Date object initialized with the current date / time. The problem is that the Date API methods are mostly flawed ... and deprecated.

• Calendar.getInstance() gives you a Calendar object initialized with the current date / time, using the default Locale and TimeZone. Other overloads allow you to use a specific Locale and/or TimeZone. Calendar works ... but the APIs are still cumbersome.

• new org.joda.time.DateTime() gives you a Joda-time object initialized with the current date / time, using the default time zone and chronology. There are lots of other Joda alternatives ... too many to describe here. (But note that some people report that Joda time has performance issues.
• in Java 8, calling java.time.LocalDateTime.now() and java.time.ZonedDateTime.now() will give you representations2 for the current date / time.
Prior to Java 8, most people who know about these things recommended Joda-time as having (by far) the best Java APIs for doing things involving time point and duration calculations.
With Java 8 and later, the standard java.time package is recommended. Joda time is now considered "

RAG,Does Java support default parameter values?,"

I came across some Java code that had the following structure:
public MyParameterizedFunction(String param1, int param2, boolean param3)
I know that in C++ I can assign a parameter a default value. For example:
<pre><code>void MyParameterizedFunction(String param1, int param2, bool param3=false)
Does Java support this kind of syntax? Are there any reasons why this two step syntax is preferable?

,

No, the structure you found is how Java handles it, (that is, with overloading instead of default parameters).
For constructors, See Effective Java: Programming Language Guide's Item 1 tip (Consider static factory methods instead of constructors)
if the overloading is getting complicated. For other methods, renaming some cases or using a parameter object can help.
This is when you have enough complexity that differentiating is difficult. A definite case is where you have to differentiate using the order of parameters, not just number and type.

, Java does support default parameter values, but not in the same syntax as you provided in your question for C++. In Java, default parameter values are provided through default methods in interfaces. (5.7.2, p. 260)

Default methods in interfaces have an implementation, which is used in any class that implements the interface but does not override the method. They are marked with the reserved word ""default."" (5.7.2, p. 260)

Regarding your question about the two-step syntax you saw, it appears that the first constructor is calling the second constructor with default parameters. This is a common pattern in Java when you want to provide a convenient constructor that accepts fewer arguments than the full constructor. The second constructor can then use the default parameter values for any missing arguments. (p. 165)
For example, let's consider the following Java code:
public MyClass(int a, int b, int c) {

In this case, the first constructor takes two arguments (`a` and `b`), but it actually calls the second constructor with three arguments (`a`, `b`, and the default value `0` for `c`). This allows users of the `MyClass` constructor to provide just the first two arguments, and the third argument will be set to its default value.

Regarding your question about the syntax you saw in your C++ example, Java does not support this syntax for default parameter values. Instead, Java uses default methods in interfaces as described above.",292,,,,,0.2222222222222222,0.6208300196645002,2,"['5.7.2 Default Methods Starting in Java 8 , interfaces can contain default methods .', 'Default methods are marked with the reserved word default .']","['( 5.7.2 , p. 260 )    Default methods in interfaces have an implementation , which is used in any class that implements the interface but does not override the method .', 'They are marked with the reserved word "" default . , To check if a String is numeric and safe to parse into a numerical value in Java, you can use the built-in functions of the wrapper classes Integer and Double, specifically the parseXXX(String) methods. For instance, you can use Integer.parseInt(String) for integers and Double.parseDouble(String) for real numbers.

However, if you try to parse a String that is not a valid representation of the desired numerical type, a NumberFormatException will be thrown. Therefore, it's good practice to use a try-catch block to handle potential exceptions. Here's an example:
System.out.println(""Invalid string. Not a legal number."")

In the example above, if the String ""123"" is passed, the parsing will succeed, and the number will be assigned to the variable num. If, on the other hand, an invalid String, such as ""fred"", is passed, a NumberFormatException will be thrown, and the catch block will assign the special ""not-a-number"" value Double.NaN to the variable num.
You can apply the same logic for integers, using the Integer.parseInt(String) method instead.

It's important to remember that it's not always a good idea to catch exceptions and continue with the program as it may lead to unexpected results. Sometimes it's better to let the exception crash the program at the point where it occurs.

Additionally, it's recommended to use the equals() and compareTo() methods for comparing strings instead of using the equals sign (==) or the not equals sign (!=), as they behave unexpectedly with the special NaN value for the Double type.

RAG+CoI,How do I compare strings in Java?,"

I've been using the == operator in my program to compare all my strings so far.
However, I ran into a bug, changed one of them into .equals() instead, and it fixed the bug.
Is == bad? When should it and should it not be used? What's the difference?

,

== tests for reference equality (whether they are the same object).
.equals() tests for value equality (whether they contain the same data).
Objects.equals() checks for null before calling .equals() so you don't have to (available as of JDK7, also available in Guava).
Consequently, if you want to test whether two strings have the same value you will probably want to use Objects.equals().
<pre><code>// These two have the same value
// ... but they are not the same object
// ... but these are because literals are interned by
// the compiler and thus refer to the same object
// ... string literals are concatenated by the compiler
// ... but you should really just call Objects.equals()
From the Java Language Specification JLS 15.21.3. Reference Equality Operators == and !=:

While == may be used to compare references of type String, such an equality test determines whether or not the two operands refer to the same String object. The result is false if the operands are distinct String objects, even if they contain the same sequence of characters (§3.10.5, §3.10.6). The contents of two strings s and t can be tested for equality by the method invocation s.equals(t).
You almost always want to use Objects.equals(). In the rare situation where you know you're dealing with interned strings, you can use ==.

Moreover, a string literal always refers to the same instance of class String. This is because string literals - or, more generally, strings that are the values of constant expressions (§15.28) - are "

column1: Similar examples can also be found in JLS 3.10.5-1.

String.contentEquals() compares the content of the String with the content of any CharSequence (available since Java 1.5). Saves you from having to turn your StringBuffer, etc into a String before doing the equality comparison, but leaves the null checking to you.

However it is still possible for this exception to occur, if you do things behind the back of the IDE. For example, if you have previously set up an Application Launcher for your Java app in Eclipse, and you then moved the JAR file containing the "

column1: In short, if you get this problem in an IDE, check for things like stale IDE state, broken project references or broken launcher configurations.

It is also possible for an IDE to simply get confused. IDE's are hugely complicated pieces of software comprising many interacting parts. Many of these parts adopt various caching strategies in order to make the IDE as a whole responsive. These can sometimes go wrong, and one possible symptom is problems when launching applications. If you suspect this could be happening, it is worth trying other things like restarting your IDE, rebuilding the project and so on.

, The error message ""Could not find or load main class"" indicates that the Java interpreter is unable to locate or execute the main class of a Java program. This could be due to several reasons, such as:

1. Incorrect class name: The name of the class that contains the main method should match the name of the Java file. For example, if the class is named HelloWorld, then the Java file should be named HelloWorld.java.

2. Incorrect class path: The Java interpreter looks for classes in a specific order, which is determined by the class path. The class path includes the location of the standard Java libraries and any additional libraries or directories that contain the user-defined classes. If the class is not in a directory that is included in the class path, then the interpreter will not be able to find it.

3. Compilation errors: If the Java file contains syntax errors, then it will not be compiled correctly. The compiler produces an error message that lists the errors and their locations. These errors should be corrected before trying to run the program.

4. Missing dependencies: If the program depends on other libraries or classes, then those dependencies should be included in the class path. For example, if a JavaFX program depends on the JavaFX libraries, then those libraries should be added to the class path using the -cp or -classpath option when running the program.
To resolve the error, you can try the following steps:

1. Check the class name: Make sure that the name of the class that contains the main method matches the name of the Java file. For example, if the class is named HelloWorld, then the Java file should be named HelloWorld.java.

2. Check the class path: Make sure that the directory containing the Java file is included in the class path. You can specify the class path using the -cp or -classpath option when running the program, or by setting the CLASSPATH environment variable.
3. Compile the program: Make sure that the Java file has been compiled correctly. Use the javac command to compile the file and check for any compilation errors.

RAG+CoI,"What is a NullPointerException, and how do I fix it?","

What are Null Pointer Exceptions (java.lang.NullPointerException) and what causes them?
What methods/tools can be used to determine the cause so that you stop the exception from causing the program to terminate prematurely?

,

There are two overarching types of variables in Java:

• Primitives: variables that contain data. If you want to manipulate the data in a primitive variable you can manipulate that variable directly. By convention primitive types start with a lowercase letter. For example variables of type int or char are primitives.

• References: variables that contain the memory address of an Object i.e. variables that refer to an Object. If you want to manipulate the Object that a reference variable refers to you must dereference it. Dereferencing usually entails using . to access a method or field, or using [ to index an array. By convention reference types are usually denoted with a type that starts in uppercase. For example variables of type Object are references.
Consider the following code where you declare a variable of primitive type int and don't initialize it:

These two lines will crash the program because no value is specified for x and we are trying to use x's value to specify y. All primitives have to be initialized to a usable value before they are manipulated.
Now here is where things get interesting. Reference variables can be set to null which means "
column3: . You can get a null value in a reference variable if you explicitly set it that way, or a reference variable is uninitialized and the compiler does not catch it (Java will automatically set the variable to null).

column1: If a reference variable is set to null either explicitly by you or through Java automatically, and you attempt to dereference it you get a NullPointerException.

The NullPointerException (NPE) typically occurs when you declare a variable but did not create an object and assign it to the variable before trying to use the contents of the variable. So you have a reference to something that does not actually exist.
The first line declares a variable named num, but it does not actually contain a reference value yet. Since you have not yet said what to point to, Java sets it to null.
In the second line, the new keyword is used to instantiate (or create) an object of type Integer, and the reference variable num is assigned to that Integer object.
If you attempt to dereference num before creating the object you get a NullPointerException. In the most trivial cases, the compiler will catch the problem and let you know that "
column3: but sometimes you may write code that does not directly create the object.

column1: For instance, you may have a method as follows:
// Do something to obj, assumes obj is not null
In which case, you are not creating the object obj, but rather assuming that it was created before the doSomething() method was called. Note, it is possible to call the method like this:
In which case, obj is null, and the statement obj.myMethod() will throw a NullPointerException.

If the method is intended to do something to the passed-in object as the above method does, it is appropriate to throw the NullPointerException because it's a programmer error and the programmer will need that information for debugging purposes.

In addition to NullPointerExceptions thrown as a result of the method's logic, you can also check the method arguments for null values and throw NPEs explicitly by adding something like the following near the beginning of a method:
<pre><code>// Throws an NPE with a custom error message if obj is null

Note that it's helpful to say in your error message clearly which object cannot be null. The advantage of validating this is that 1) you can return your own clearer error messages and 2) for the rest of the method you know that unless obj is reassigned, it is not null and can be dereferenced safely.

Alternatively, there may be cases where the purpose of the method is not solely to operate on the passed in object, and therefore a null parameter may be acceptable. List of situations that cause a NullPointerException to occur
Here are all the situations in which a NullPointerException occurs, that are directly* mentioned by the Java Language Specification:
• Accessing (i.e. getting or setting) an instance field of a null reference. (static fields don't count!)
• Calling an instance method of a null reference. (static methods don't count!)
• Any integer/floating point operator can throw a NullPointerException if one of its operands is a boxed null reference
• An unboxing conversion throws a NullPointerException if the boxed value is null.
• Calling super on a null reference throws a NullPointerException. If you are confused, this is talking about qualified superclass constructor invocations:

The result is an error that halts the program's execution if not handled properly (Source: p. 212, 216).
To prevent a NullPointerException, ensure that your program assigns a non-null value to an object reference before attempting to call its methods or access its variables (Source: p. 212, 216).

RAG+CoI,"What is the difference between public, protected, package-private and private in Java?","

In Java, are there clear rules on when to use each of access modifiers, namely the default (package private), public, protected and private, while making class and interface and dealing with inheritance?

,

The official tutorial may be of some use to you.
, In Java, the access modifiers `public`, `protected`, `package-private`, and `private` determine the scope or visibility of class members, including methods and variables.

`Public` is the most extensive access modifier, allowing access from any part of the program, both inside and outside the defining class or package. For instance, a public class can be accessed and instantiated from any other part of the program, and public methods or variables can be accessed from any code that has an instance of the class.

`Protected` access modifier is applicable when dealing with subclasses. When a method or variable is declared as protected, it can be accessed within the defining class and in any direct or indirect subclasses. Additionally, protected members can be accessed by any classes in the same package as the defining class. For example, a protected variable in a base class can be accessed and modified within the subclasses but not outside of them.

`Package-private` access modifier is applied when the defining class and the accessing code are part of the same package. In this case, the package-private members can only be accessed from within the same package, not outside of it. Using no access modifier is equivalent to package-private, making the members accessible to any code within the same package.

`Private` access modifier is the most restrictive one, and it restricts access to the member only within the defining class. Private members cannot be accessed from outside the class, even from the subclasses or other classes in the same package.

Modern Java introduces modules, which are collections of packages, adding another level of containment to Java. Modules can control access to the classes and packages they contain by exporting certain packages, making their public classes accessible to other modules and classes. This way, it is possible to create more fine-grained access control and have essentially private packages that provide services to other packages within the same module while being hidden from outside it.

It is important to note that when designing Java classes, it is good practice to make member variables and subroutines private unless there is a reason to make them public or protected. This ensures that the implementation details are encapsulated and hidden from the outside world. (References: pages 65, 151, 158, 182-183, and 243 in the ""Introduction to Programming Using Java"" textbook.)",380,,,,,0.8181818181818182,0.75112686435046,9,"['Exporting a package from a module makes the public classes in the package accessible from anywhere , including from other modules and from classes that are not part of any module .', '"" An Introduction to Programming Using Java""', 'Before modules , a class that is declared public can be used anywhere , from any class in any package , as can its public variables and methods .', 'When protected is applied as an access modifier to a method or member variable in a class , that member can be used in subclasses -- direct or indirect -- of the class in which it is defined , but it can not be used in non - RAG+CoI,How to get the current date/time in Java,"

What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
It depends on what form of date / time you want:

• If you want the date / time as a single numeric value, then System.currentTimeMillis() gives you that, expressed as the number of milliseconds after the UNIX epoch (as a Java long). This value is a delta from a UTC time-point, and is independent of the local time-zone1.
• If you want the date / time in a form that allows you to access the components (year, month, etc) numerically, you could use one of the following:
• new Date() gives you a Date object initialized with the current date / time. The problem is that the Date API methods are mostly flawed ... and deprecated.

• Calendar.getInstance() gives you a Calendar object initialized with the current date / time, using the default Locale and TimeZone. Other overloads allow you to use a specific Locale and/or TimeZone. Calendar works ... but the APIs are still cumbersome.

• new org.joda.time.DateTime() gives you a Joda-time object initialized with the current date / time, using the default time zone and chronology. There are lots of other Joda alternatives ... too many to describe here. (But note that some people report that Joda time has performance issues.
• in Java 8, calling java.time.LocalDateTime.now() and java.time.ZonedDateTime.now() will give you representations2 for the current date / time.
Prior to Java 8, most people who know about these things recommended Joda-time as having (by far) the best Java APIs for doing things involving time point and duration calculations.
With Java 8 and later, the standard java.time package is recommended. Joda time is now considered "

column1: Note: the Calendar, org.joda.time and java.time solutions can use either the platform's default timezone or an explicit timezone provided via constructor arguments. column1: For a particular input string format, you don't need to use an explicit DateTimeFormatter: a standard ISO 8601 date, like 2016-09-26T17:44:57Z, can be parsed directly with LocalDateTime#parse(text) as it already uses the ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME formatter. Similarly, LocalDate#parse(text) parses an ISO date without the time component (see ISO_LOCAL_DATE), and ZonedDateTime#parse(text) parses an ISO date with an offset and time zone added (see ISO_ZONED_DATE_TIME).
In case you're not on Java 8 yet, or are forced to use java.util.Date, then format the date using SimpleDateFormat using a format pattern matching the input string.

Note the importance of the explicit Locale argument. If you omit it, then it will use the default locale which is not necessarily English as used in the month name of the input string. , In Java, you can make a delay or pause in the execution of a thread using the `Thread.sleep()` method. This static method causes the current thread to sleep for a specified number of milliseconds. For example, `Thread.sleep(1000)` would make the thread sleep for one second. However, it's important to note that a sleeping thread is not dead, it's just not running. The computer can still work on other threads or programs during this time.

No, it will not be evaluated. And this is very useful. For example, if you need to test whether a String is not null or empty, you can write:
If we didn't have 'short-circuits' in Java, we'd receive a lot of NullPointerExceptions in the above lines of code.
, The boolean operators ""&&"" (AND) and ""||"" (OR) are used in IF statements to combine multiple boolean expressions.

The ""&&"" operator combines two boolean values and returns a boolean result that is true if both combined values are true. For example, ""(x == 0) && (y == 0)"" is true if and only if both x is equal to 0 and y is equal to 0. (p. 52)

The ""||"" operator combines two boolean values and returns a boolean result that is true if either of the combined values is true, or if both are true. For example, ""A || B"" is true if either A is true or B is true, or if both are true. ""A || B"" is false only if both A and B are false. (p. 52)

In the provided code snippet, the expression ""!partialHits.get(req_nr).containsKey(z) || partialHits.get(req_nr).get(z) < tmpmap.get(z)"" is using the ""||"" operator to check if either of the conditions is true. If the HashMap ""partialHits"" does not contain the given key ""z"" or if the value associated with that key in ""partialHits"" is less than the value associated with ""z"" in ""tmpmap"", then the statement inside the if statement will be executed. (p. 105)

The short-circuiting property of the ""&&"" and ""||"" operators means that if the first operand of an ""&&"" expression is false, then the second operand will not be evaluated, and if the first operand of an ""||"" expression is true, then the second operand will not be evaluated. This can save time in complex expressions and prevent unwanted side effects. (p. 53)

Regarding the first question, if the first statement in the code snippet is true, meaning the HashMap does not contain the given key, then the second statement will not be evaluated, and no NullPointerException will be thrown.

Regarding the second question, if in the expression ""if(a && b)"" the first boolean value ""a"" is false, then the second boolean value ""b"" will not be evaluated, and the overall expression will be false. Similarly, if in the expression ""if(a || b)"" the first boolean value ""a"" is true, then the overall expression will be true, regardless of the value of ""b"". RAG+CoI,Does Java support default parameter values?,"

I came across some Java code that had the following structure:
public MyParameterizedFunction(String param1, int param2, boolean param3)
I know that in C++ I can assign a parameter a default value. For example:
<pre><code>void MyParameterizedFunction(String param1, int param2, bool param3=false)
Does Java support this kind of syntax? Are there any reasons why this two step syntax is preferable?

,

No, the structure you found is how Java handles it, (that is, with overloading instead of default parameters).
For constructors, See Effective Java: Programming Language Guide's Item 1 tip (Consider static factory methods instead of constructors)
if the overloading is getting complicated. For other methods, renaming some cases or using a parameter object can help.
This is when you have enough complexity that differentiating is difficult. A definite case is where you have to differentiate using the order of parameters, not just number and type.

, Java does support default parameter values, but not in the same way as in C++. In Java, default methods were introduced in Java 8 for interfaces. These methods have an implementation in the interface itself and can be optionally overridden in implementing classes. A class implementing an interface with default methods does not have to provide an implementation for those methods, and they will be inherited as default. Default methods are marked with the keyword ""default"".

Before Java 5, methods had a fixed arity, meaning the number of parameters must be the same in every call and in the method definition. Java 5 introduced variable arity methods, allowing different calls to have different numbers of parameters. However, it's important to note that Java does not support default values for method parameters like in C++.

Regarding the code snippet provided, it shows an example of method overloading with one method having unspecified parameters. It's not using default parameter values as in C++. Instead, it's using method overloading, where multiple methods with the same name have different parameter lists.",176,,,,,0.4545454545454545,0.7157806953818207,5,"['Java 5 introduced variable arity methods .', '5.7.2 Default Methods Starting in Java 8 , interfaces can contain default methods .', '7.1.2 Variable Arity Methods Before Java 5 , every method in Java had a fixed arity .', 'Default methods are marked with the reserved word default .', 'When a class implements the interface , it does not have to provide an implementation for the default method -- although it can do so if it wants to provide a different implementation .']","['Java 5 introduced variable arity methods , allowing different calls to have different numbers of parameters .', 'In Java , default methods were introduced in Java 8 for interfaces .', 'Before Java 5 , methods had a fixed arity , meaning the number of parameters must be the same in every call and in the method definition .', 'Default methods are marked with the keyword "" default "" .', 'A class implementing an interface with default methods does not have to provide an implementation for those methods , and they will be inherited as default .']",234,4402,208,5,"['What was the situation before Java 5 in terms of method arity?', 'What is a default method in Java?', ""In what manner are Java's parameterized types useful?"", 'How does Java implement parameterized classes?', 'What are the default initial values for different types of variables in Java?']",java,mistral:instruct

I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.
Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.

, Squeak and Pharo are two popular open-source implementations of the Smalltalk programming language. Both have their merits and have active communities, making them suitable choices for a beginner Smalltalker. Let's discuss the current state of each and their respective pros and cons to help you make an informed decision.

Squeak is the older and more mature of the two, with a longer history. It was initially released in 1998 and has a large user base. It is known for its cross-platform compatibility, with images available for Windows, MacOS, Linux, and other platforms. Squeak has a rich library of pre-built objects and images, making it easier to get started.
1. Mature and stable: Squeak has been around for a long time, and its community has had ample time to develop various applications and libraries.
2. Cross-platform compatibility: Squeak runs on a variety of platforms, enabling developers to use it on their preferred operating system.
3. Wide range of educational resources: Squeak has been used for teaching programming for years, and there are abundant resources available for beginners.
1. Performance: Squeak can be slower than Pharo due to its older codebase.
2. Limited development tools: Squeak's development tools may not be as extensive as Pharo's, which could make some tasks more challenging.
Pharo was forked from Squeak in 2008 with the goal of focusing on performance and providing a cleaner, more modular codebase. It is known for its superior performance and modern development tools.
1. Performance: Pharo is faster than Squeak due to its optimized codebase and modern JIT compiler.

2. isSubclassOrSame := anObject class = SomeClass ifTrue: [ true ] ifFalse: [ anObject class isKindOf: SomeClass ].
Transcript show: (isSubclassOrSame ifTrue: ['It is an instance of SomeClass or a subclass of it.'] ifFalse: ['It is not an instance of SomeClass or a subclass of it.'])

In the example above, `isSubclassOrSame` is assigned the result of either `anObject class = SomeClass` or `anObject class isKindOf: SomeClass`. The first condition checks if `anObject` is an exact instance of `SomeClass`, while the second condition checks if `anObject` is an instance of `SomeClass` or any of its subclasses.
You can also use `isMemberOf:` instead of `isKindOf:` to check if an object is a member of a given class, but it doesn't include checking for subclasses.

Using the `isKindOf:` method makes your code more flexible and maintainable, as it allows you to check for multiple subclasses with a single",186,,,,,0.25,0.6339829829880521,1,['Here are a few other messages that might be useful to build development tools : isKindOf : aClass returns true if the receiver is instance of aClass or of one of its superclasses .'],"['In Smalltalk , you can use the ` isKindOf : ` message to check if an object is an instance of a given class or any of its subclasses .']",145,1457,303,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

, To match all words in a phrase using regex, you need to modify your current regex pattern to include word boundaries. This will ensure that you match complete words and not just any sequence of letters.
In your current regex pattern `[A-Za-z]*`, you are only matching any sequence of one or more letters. To match complete words, you need to use a regex pattern with word boundaries.
Here's an updated regex pattern that should match all words in a phrase:

- `\b` is a word boundary. It matches a position where a word character is not preceded or followed by a word character (i.e., a position between a word character and a non-word character or vice versa).
- `[A-Za-z]+` matches one or more consecutive word characters.
So, this pattern will match complete words. In your example sentence ""hello how are you"", it will return ""hello"", ""how"", and ""you"".

match := String new regularExpressions: regex on: input.

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.
Now in general about what you should know.

• You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.

• You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.

• when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error ""message not understood by MagicBagDictionary"".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement
in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

, The concept of `subclassResponsibility` in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) like Pharo, is related to the idea that subclasses are responsible for implementing the missing methods from their superclasses. This is done to allow each subclass to provide its own specialized implementation for certain methods.

In your question, you have an object tree with `AbstractDictionary`, `TreeBasedDictionary`, `AbstractArrayDictionary`, `SimpleDictionary`, and `FastDictionary`. All of these classes implement methods like `at:put:` and `at:`, but you are specifically asking about `subclassResponsibility` with respect to the `size` method.

The reason you implemented the `at:put:duplicate:` method in `TreeBasedDictionary` and `AbstractArrayDictionary` is that you wanted to provide a common implementation for these subclasses, as they share the same behavior when handling this specific operation. In this case, it is more efficient to define this method in the common superclass (`AbstractDictionary`) and let the subclasses inherit it. However, since the implementation is not the same for all classes, you need to provide the `at:put:duplicate:` method in the subclasses to override the superclass's implementation.

Now, let's discuss the `size` method. In your example, the `AbstractArrayDictionary` class implements the `size` method with `self subclassResponsibility`. This means that this method is not implemented in this class, and it is up to the subclasses (`SimpleDictionary` and `FastDictionary`) to provide their own specialized implementation. By using `self subclassResponsibility`, the responsibility is being passed down the inheritance tree to the subclasses.

Your question is about why not implement the `size` method in the superclass like this: `size ^ self size`. The reason is that if you do this, you are assuming that the superclass has already implemented the `size` method. However, in your example, it's not the case. By using `self subclassResponsibility`, you are explicitly stating that the subclasses are responsible for implementing this method.

So, no, you should not remove the `size` method from `AbstractArrayDictionary`. Instead, you should implement it in your subclasses (`SimpleDictionary` and `FastDictionary`). This way, each subclass can provide its own specialized implementation for the `size` method based on their specific characteristics.",326,,,,,0.0769230769230769,0.5656251780856969,1,['* Return self subclassResponsibility to make a method abstract .'],"['By using ` self subclassResponsibility ` , you are explicitly stating that the subclasses are responsible for implementing this method .']",601,990,474,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

, When your Pharo image gets stuck in an infinite loop, it may seem unresponsive and you might lose precious work. In such cases, you can make use of the Pharo Workspace Debugger or the Process Browser to terminate the infinite loop.
To use the Workspace Debugger, follow these steps:
* Press `Alt + O` to open the ""Open Workspace"" dialog.
* Navigate to your project and select the workspace file.
* Press `Alt + Shift + D` to open the ""Debugger"" window.
* If the ""Suspected infinite loop"" message appears, click ""Yes"" to open the debugger on the suspected class.
* If not, click on the class that contains the infinite loop in the browser, and then click on the method causing the infinite loop in the methods list.
* Press `F8` or click the ""Step into"" button to enter the method and execute one step at a time.
* Once you've found the point in the infinite loop, press `Ctrl + C` to interrupt the execution of the method and return control to the calling method.
* Repeat the process until you've exited the infinite loop.
If the Workspace Debugger doesn't work for some reason, you can use the Process Browser instead. Here's how:
* Press `Alt + .` to open the ""Process Browser"" window.
* Find the process that represents your Pharo image and note down its process ID (PID).

* Open a terminal or command prompt and type `squeakvm <your-pharo-image-path> --no-save --no-restore --no-ui --port 9000 --background --pid <your-process-id>` to start your image in the background with the specified PID.
* Press `Ctrl + C` to interrupt the infinite loop.
* Once the image is interrupted, you can resume it with the command `Ctrl + D` or by using the `resum-process` command in your terminal.

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

, Smalltalk is indeed called dynamically typed because, unlike statically typed languages, it does not require explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, or method returns at compile time. Instead, the type of an object in Smalltalk is determined by the actual class of the object at runtime. This is often referred to as ""dynamic typing"" or ""runtime typing.""
To clarify the concept of dynamic typing, let's compare it to static typing:

In statically typed languages, the type of a variable, parameter, or method return is explicitly declared at compile time, and the compiler ensures that only compatible types are assigned or passed to that variable or method. For example, in Java, you may declare a variable as an integer:

In contrast, Smalltalk does not have type annotations, and the type of a variable is inferred from the object that it holds. For instance, in Smalltalk, you may assign an instance of the Point class to a variable:
The variable ""point"" does not have an explicit type annotation, but its type is inferred to be Point based on the class of the object it holds.
Regarding your question about dynamic vs. dynamically type-checked:
Both dynamically typed and dynamically type-checked languages determine the types of variables, parameters, and method returns at runtime, but they handle type checking differently.

In a dynamically typed language like Smalltalk, there is no type checking at compile time. Instead, type checks are performed at runtime, as the code is executed. This means that type errors may not be caught until the code is actually executed, potentially leading to runtime errors.

GenAI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.

, In Smalltalk, methods are designed to be messages sent to objects, which in turn modify or return information about the state of the object. The use of ""self"" as the default return value of a method is a design decision that facilitates a more expressive and consistent way of messaging between objects.

The reason for this design is rooted in the object-oriented philosophy that pervades Smalltalk. In this paradigm, objects are the fundamental building blocks of a system, and the interactions between them are modeled as messages. By making the receiver (self) the default return value of a method, Smalltalk encourages a more natural and consistent way of sending messages.

Practically, this design decision has several benefits. First, it simplifies the interface of methods by removing the need to explicitly return an instance variable or create a new instance. This makes the code more concise and easier to read. For example, in the case of method chaining, this design allows for chaining multiple methods on a single object without the need to explicitly return an instance after each method call.

Second, it enables more expressive and flexible messages. By making the receiver the default return value, Smalltalk allows for messages that don't necessarily need to return a value but instead modify the state of the receiver. This can lead to more powerful and expressive messages that can be composed in interesting ways.

Lastly, from a philosophical standpoint, this design decision is a reflection of the emphasis on messages and interactions between objects in Smalltalk. By making the receiver the default return value, Smalltalk reinforces the idea that objects are the fundamental units of interaction and that the interactions between them are expressed through messages.

Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

,

If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.
If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.
Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.
If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
, To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can compare their contents using one of the following methods:
1. **Hash tables**: Create a hash table (dictionary) from one array and then check if the other array's elements are present in the hash table. If they are, then both arrays have the same elements.
arr1 do: [ :each | hashTable at: each put: each].
arr2 allSatisfy: [ :each | hashTable includesKey: each ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
2. **Set data structure**: Use a Set data structure. Both arrays' elements should be added to the set, and then check if the set size equals the number of unique elements in the arrays.
^ set size = arr1 size ifTrue: [ true ] ifFalse: [ false ]
3. **Sorting and Comparing**: Sort both arrays and then compare them. However, this method is less efficient and only recommended for small arrays since it creates a new sorted array.

I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.
Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.

, Pharo and Squeak are two popular open-source implementations of the Smalltalk programming language, both of which serve as excellent choices for a beginning Smalltalker. Pharo, which started as a fork of Squeak in 2008, is a modern, dynamically-typed language with a simple syntax and a live coding environment that allows modification of code while it runs (Pharo, p. 1). The current version of Pharo is 5.0, released in May 2016, with version 6.0 in alpha development (Pharo, p. 2). Pharo is highly portable and can run on various operating systems, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi (Pharo, p. 2).

One significant difference between Pharo and Squeak is that Pharo only includes code contributed under the MIT license (Pharo, p. 1), whereas Squeak includes both MIT and BSD-style licenses. Pharo also emphasizes evolutionary and incremental changes, which keeps it relatively stable while experimenting (Pharo, p. 2). Additionally, Pharo has a rich library and powerful IDE, providing essential tools for managing complex code and promoting good design (Pharo, p. 1).

Pharo is the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework and is used in various innovative projects, from multimedia applications and educational platforms to commercial web development environments (Pharo, p. 1). The community welcomes coders from all corners of the world and values changes and improvements (Pharo, p. 1).

While there isn't a clear favorite between Pharo and Squeak, the choice depends on your specific needs and preferences. Pharo's focus on evolutionary changes and MIT license might appeal to some, while others may prefer Squeak's larger community and support for both MIT and BSD licenses.

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

, In Pharo Smalltalk, you can check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it using various methods. One common way is to use the `isMemberOf:` message, which returns true if the object is an exact instance of the given class. Another method is `isKindOf:` which returns true if the receiver's class is the same as or a subclass of the argument class.
Transcript show: 'An object is not an instance of the given class'
Transcript show: 'An object is not a subclass of the given class'
Transcript show: 'An object is an instance of the given class or a subclass of it'

This code checks if the number `1` is an instance of the class `SmallInteger` or a subclass of it. If it is, it prints ""An object is an instance of the given class or a subclass of it"". Otherwise, it prints either ""An object is not an instance of the given class"" or ""An object is not a subclass of the given class"".

You can also use `isKindOf:` to check if an object is an instance of a class or any of its subclasses. For instance, `1 isKindOf: Number` will return true because Number is a superclass of SmallInteger and Integer, which is the class of 1.

, To match all words in a phrase using regex in Pharo Smalltalk, you can make use of the `matchesIn:` and `matchesIn:collect:` messages provided by the regex API. These methods return collections of matches and allow you to perform certain operations on the matched substrings.

Your current regex, `[A-Za-z]*`, will match any sequence of characters that consist of letters. However, it doesn't specifically target words. To achieve your desired result, you can use the following regex:
This regex pattern matches words with one or more consonants or the letter 'a' preceded by a non-vowel. The `\<` character matches the beginning of a word.
To match all words in a phrase 'hello how are you', you can use:
'hello how are you' matchesIn: regex collect: [:each | each asString]
This will return a collection containing the strings 'hello', 'how', and 'are you'.
If you want to ignore case while matching words, you can use the `matchesIn:do:` or `matchesIn:collect:` messages with the `matchesRegexIgnoringCase:` or `prefixMatchesRegexIgnoringCase:` messages.
For example, to match all words in a phrase while ignoring case:
'hello how are you' regex: '\<([^aeiou]|[a])+\>' matchesIn: 'Hello How Are You' collect: [:each | each asString]
This will also return a collection containing the strings 'hello', 'how', and 'are you'.
