In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

,

The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.
meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.
The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.
Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
which is a little bit more verbose.
To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
, In Smalltalk, both `self` and `yourself` represent the receiver of a message, but they behave differently in method execution and method lookup.

The `self` keyword refers to the object that receives the message and initiates the method execution. Method lookup starts in the class of the receiver. On the other hand, `yourself` is less commonly used and it is equivalent to explicitly returning the receiver from a method. In the absence of an explicit return statement, a method returns the receiver as its result.
When you explicitly write `^ self`, it is not a good practice as it communicates that you are returning something of interest to the sender, but in this case, you're simply returning the receiver.
There is also a concept of `super` in Smalltalk, which might lead to confusion. Unlike common belief, `super` does not refer to the superclass
column2: instead, it refers to the receiver of the message in the context of a superclass method. The difference lies in the method lookup: instead of starting in the class of the receiver, method lookup begins in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs.

column1: For instance, consider the following example:

In this example, `self` is bound to the respective receiver, either `WebServer` or `ReliableWebServer`. Using `self` ensures that the correct class is returned when creating an instance. `super` does not exist in this context, but if it did, the lookup would start in the superclass `Object` instead of `WebServer`.

Additionally, it is important to note that Pharo uses the receiver to perform method lookup and calls, so `self` is a pseudo-variable representing the receiver and is called the ""receiver of a message.""
Confusion may arise when comparing `self` and `yourself` to `super` and `superclass`. In summary:
- `self`: Represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver.
- `yourself`: Equivalent to explicitly returning the receiver from a method.
- `super`: Represents the receiver of the message in the context of a superclass method, and method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs.

RAG,Smalltalk initialization variables,"

In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.
How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?
Or is this bad practice and should I always do

,

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
• You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.
There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

, In Pharo Smalltalk, you don't initialize instance variables using a constructor with parameters like in languages such as Java or C++. Instead, you can initialize an instance variable directly in the class side, using the `initialize` class method or lazy initialization. This is because Pharo manages the object lifecycle differently, and users typically expect the class to be ""ready"" to return new instances without having to call initialization methods explicitly.

When defining a new instance variable, it's recommended to avoid initializing it in accessor methods, unless you have a specific reason for doing so. Instead, you can initialize it directly in the class side, as shown below:

Here, we define a new class `MyClass` and initialize its instance variable `myVariable` to 42 using the `initializeMyVariable:` class method in the `initialize` class method. Then, when creating a new instance of `MyClass`, the `initialize` method will be called automatically, and the `myVariable` instance variable will be initialized accordingly.

Pharo provides three types of shared variables: globally shared variables, class variables, and pool variables. Global variables are stored in the `Smalltalk` instance of the `SystemDictionary` class and are accessible everywhere. Class variables are shared between instances and classes, while pool variables are shared among a group of classes.

To define a global variable, you can simply assign a value to an undeclared identifier, and the parser will offer to declare it for you. To define a global programmatically, you can use the `Smalltalk globals at:` and `put:` messages. Class variables are initialized in the `initialize` class method, using lazy initialization or initialization when an instance is created, depending on the specific use case.
Regarding your question about initializing a `Color` object with a specific color name, you can create a new `Color` instance and set its name property using the dot (.) notation:
Alternatively, you can use the class method `Color red` to create a new `Color` instance with the 'red' name already set:

Both methods achieve the same result. The choice between them depends on your personal preference and the specific use case.",376,,,,,0.5,0.7034536597997433,7,"['The usual way to define a global is just to perform Do it on an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier .', 'To create an instance of the class Color one can use the method r : g : b : ( as in Color r : 1 g : 0 b : 0 ) , which creates the color red .', 'If you want to define a global programmatically , just execute Smalltalk globals at : # AGlobalName put : nil .', 'Although it may be tempting to instead initialize instance variables in their respective accessor methods ( using ifNil : checks ) , avoid this unless you have a good reason .', 'Pharo provides three kinds of shared variables : 1 .', 'Global variables In Pharo , all global variables are stored in a namespace called Smalltalk ,', 'Pool variables Pool variables are variables that are shared between several classes that may not be related by inheritance .']","['To define a global variable , you can simply assign a value to an undeclared identifier , and the parser will offer to declare it for you .', ""` ` `    Alternatively , you can use the class method ` Color red ` to create a new ` Color ` instance with the ' red ' name already set :    ` ` ` smalltalk   | aRedColor |   aRedColor : = Color red ."", 'To define a global programmatically , you can use the ` Smalltalk globals at : ` and ` put : ` messages .', 'Instead , you can initialize an instance variable directly in the class side , using the ` initialize ` class method or lazy initialization .', ""When defining a new instance variable , it 's recommended to avoid initializing it in accessor methods , unless you have a specific reason for doing so ."", 'Pharo provides three types of shared variables : globally shared variables , class variables , and pool variables .', 'Global variables are stored in the ` Smalltalk ` instance of the ` SystemDictionary ` class and are accessible everywhere .', 'Class variables are shared between instances and classes , while pool variables are shared among a group of classes .']",179,1409,537,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

RAG,For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk,"

I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->
1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter
It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.
2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'
For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)
The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.
4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error
<pre><code>  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).
There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:
the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.
5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random
The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been
6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with ""toy"" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)

, The issue with your code (p. 1) is that you're trying to put values into an Array starting from index 1, but you're using the message ""put:"" instead of ""at:put:"". In Pharo Smalltalk, arrays are zero-indexed, but when you create a new array using ""Array new: 31"" or initialize an array with a fixed size, the first element is at index 1, not 0.
To make your code work, you should modify it to use the ""at:"" message to access and update the elements of the array. Here's an example of how you can modify your code:
1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom].

Additionally, it's important to note that when you're using a loop to iterate over an array or a collection, you should use the message ""do:"" instead of ""to:do:"" to avoid confusion. ""do:"" is the basic collection iterator that applies its argument (a block) to each element of the receiver. Here's an example of how you can use ""do:"" to print the elements of an array:

By using ""do:"" instead of ""to:do:"", you avoid the confusion of using ""to:"" as an index, and it also makes your code more robust and compact, as the Smalltalk community follows the convention of using high-level messages like ""do:"" for iterating over collections.

RAG,How to access class variables in Smalltalk,"

I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.
I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.
Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute.
Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time.
In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:
Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?

,

Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).
In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.
And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:
If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:
Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.

, To access a class variable in Smalltalk, such as the year variable in Class1, which is shared among all instances of Class1 and the class itself, you cannot directly access it using the dot notation as you would with instance variables. Instead, Smalltalk provides class methods to access class variables.

In the context of your question, since Class1 has class variables year, month, day, hour, minute, and both Class1 and Class2 are interacting with each other, you can define class methods in Class1 to set and get the class variables' values.
First, let's define a class method in Class1 to set the year:
Now, in Class2's initialize method, you can call the setYear class method to assign the year 2012 to start-time:
To get the value of the year class variable, you can define a class method in Class1:
Now, in Class2, you can access the year class variable using the class method:
Transcript show: 'The year of start-time is: ', yearOfStartTime.

Keep in mind that class variables are shared among all instances and the class itself. Therefore, changing the year class variable's value in Class1 will affect all instances of Class1 and the class itself.
You can apply similar steps to set and get other class variables in Class1 if needed.",246,,,,,0.0,0.5393271272987117,0,[],[],218,1378,373,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

RAG,Pharo method with multiple arguments,"

I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error ""Variable or expression expected..""
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :
<pre><code>    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

,

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well
column2: ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)

column1: , To create a Pharo method with three arguments, you should use keyword message syntax with the colon `:` separator for each argument. The correct method declaration syntax is:
MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
For example, you can define a method named `myMethod` as follows:
MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
Later on, you can call this method with three arguments using the same syntax:
ClassName new myMethod: argValue1 argValue2: argValue2 argValue3: argValue3
This syntax is described on pages 64 and 65 of ""Pharo By Example"" by Stéphane Ducasse, Dimitris Chloupis, Nicolai Hess, and Dmitri Zagidulin.

Regarding your initial error, the `arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2` syntax is incorrect because it is trying to define a method with two arguments named `arg1` and `argValue1`, and another argument named `arg2` and `argValue2`. In the corrected syntax, all arguments have their own names.

Additionally, Pharo uses a messaging system instead of method invocation. When you send a message to an object, it decides which method to execute based on the message name and its methods. This concept is described on page 100 of the textbook.",184,,,,,0.2857142857142857,0.6065110103708876,2,"['When sending a message to an object , the object , and not the sender , selects the appropriate method for responding to your message .', 'Pharo is based on the concept of sending messages to objects .']","['When you send a message to an object , it decides which method to execute based on the message name and its methods .', 'Additionally , Pharo uses a messaging system instead of method invocation .']",217,1372,330,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.
Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.
Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:
With these three methods you should be able to run your example.
Some additional comments on the coding style:
Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the ""constructor"" method (in Smalltalk we call these ""instance creation"" methods). For instance,
Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.
Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in
which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.

, The issue with the given code is that the constructor method `newName:` and `newAge:` should be defined as class methods instead of instance methods. In Smalltalk, including Pharo, constructors are typically implemented as class methods with the same name as the class, prefixed with the `new` keyword.
^ self new initialize: aName put: 'name' put: aAge put: nil.
^ self new initialize: aName put: 'name' put: aAge put: nil.

In the corrected version, I've added a class method `new:` which accepts a dictionary and initializes the new instance using the given keys and values. I've also updated the constructor method to be a class method, and I've added a class method `new:` that uses the constructor method to create new instances. Finally, I've updated the initialize method to accept a dictionary and initialize the instance variables accordingly.
Now you can create a new instance using the following syntax:
This should work correctly in Pharo, as it will call the `newName:` class method to create a new instance and initialize it with the given arguments.
RAG,SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript,"<pre><code>|oc|
Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:
When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.
Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?

,

Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

, The error you are encountering, ""MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript,"" occurs because Transcript is not a recognized receiver for the message ""Transcript"" in the context of an OrderedCollection object.

In Pharo Smalltalk, Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages and serves as a kind of system console. However, it's not designed to be a receiver for messages related to collections like OrderedCollection. Instead, you should use methods provided by OrderedCollection or other classes for processing and printing the contents of your collection.

For instance, you can use the 'printString' message to display the contents of an OrderedCollection by iterating through its elements and adding their string representations to a String object using 'nextPutAll:' or 'print:'. Here's an example of how to do it:
[ oc do: [ :each | string nextPutAll: [ each printString ], space ].

This code snippet creates an OrderedCollection, adds some elements to it, and then iterates through the collection, adding each element's string representation to a String object, followed by a space character. Finally, it prints the string representation of the entire OrderedCollection in the Transcript.

If you want to create a collection using a WriteStream, you should read the 'Streaming over collections' and 'Writing to collections' sections from your textbook (pp. 226-228). These sections provide detailed examples of how to create and manipulate collections using WriteStreams.",239,,,,,0.2,0.6433783759926077,2,"[""We 'll now learn how to create collections using WriteStream s."", 'The Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages .']","[""If you want to create a collection using a WriteStream , you should read the ' Streaming over collections ' and ' Writing to collections ' sections from your textbook ( pp .)"", 'In Pharo Smalltalk , Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages and serves as a kind of system console .', 'These sections provide detailed examples of how to create and manipulate collections using WriteStreams .']",216,1382,347,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

RAG,How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?,"

I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

,

You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own ""if"" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.
E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
, To write if-elseif-elseif conditions in Pharo, you should express conditionals by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments. Pharo does not have special syntax for control constructs
column2: instead, these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections (Pharo by Example, p. 66).

column1: Here's an example of writing if-elseif-elseif conditions in Pharo:
If x is less than 25, DoSomething1 will be executed. If x is equal to 25, DoSomething2 will be executed. Otherwise, DoSomething3 will be executed.

Each condition is checked by sending the ifTrue: message to the result of a boolean expression. These messages are inlined by the compiler and transformed to comparison and jump bytecodes, as described in Pharo by Example, p. 66.
Keep in mind that the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue: message also require the use of square brackets since we do not know how many times they will be executed.
For example, instead of using a traditional loop like:
You should use the Pharo idiom:
[ i rangeDo: [ :index | index = i ] whileTrue: [ i := i + 1 ] ]

This idiom sends the message rangeDo: to the collection of integers from 1 to 10, followed by the whileTrue: message. The receiver and argument of whileTrue: are both blocks, so they require the use of square brackets.

In summary, to write if-elseif-elseif conditions in Pharo, express conditionals by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments. Remember that the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue: message also require the use of square brackets.",294,,,,,0.4444444444444444,0.6641722747499776,4,"['For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .', '4.6 Conditionals and loops in a nutshell Pharo offers no special syntax for control constructs .', 'Some conditionals Conditionals are expressed by sending one of the messages ifTrue : ,', 'Similarly , both the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue : message require the use of square brackets since we do not know how many times either the receiver ( the loop conditional ) or the argument ( the "" loop body "" ) will be executed .']","['To write if - elseif - elseif conditions in Pharo , you should express conditionals by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments .', 'Pharo does not have special syntax for control constructs

RAG,Usage of self subclassResponsibility,"

I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.
All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows
Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.
My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:
And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.
Now in general about what you should know.

• You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.

• You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
<li>It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
• when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).

• when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error ""message not understood by MagicBagDictionary"".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement
in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:
yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.

, The concept of `subclassResponsibility` in Pharo is a way for superclasses to announce that subclasses should implement certain methods. When a subclass does not implement a method announced by its superclass using `subclassResponsibility`, an error will be raised when the method is called.

In your object tree, you have `AbstractDictionary` as a superclass, and `TreeBasedDictionary` and `AbstractArrayDictionary` as subclasses. All three classes implement methods like `at:put:` and `at:`, but they might have different implementations or additional methods, such as `size`.

When you call `at:put:` on an instance of `TreeBasedDictionary` or `AbstractArrayDictionary`, it won't find the method in that instance, but it will find it in the superclass `AbstractDictionary`. This is because method lookup follows the inheritance chain, and super sends start the method lookup in the superclass of the class in which the super send is written.

When it comes to the `size` method, `AbstractArrayDictionary` implements it as `self subclassResponsibility`, which signals that the subclass (`TreeBasedDictionary` in this case) should have implemented this method. However, you might wonder why not just implement `size` in the superclass and call it down the inheritance tree.

The reason for this design is that it allows for flexibility and customization in the subclasses. If you implement `size` in the superclass and call it down the inheritance tree, you might end up with inconsistent or incorrect implementations. By using `subclassResponsibility`, the subclasses have the responsibility to define their concrete versions of the method.

Regarding your question about why you cannot just call `size` as `^self size`, it's essential to understand that in this context, `size` is an abstract method. Abstract methods are methods that are intended to be implemented by subclasses, and they are typically declared using the `subclassResponsibility` mechanism. In your example, `AbstractArrayDictionary` announces that it should have implemented the `size` method, and it's the responsibility of the subclasses (`TreeBasedDictionary` and `FastDictionary`) to define their concrete versions of the method.

So, if you try to call `size` as `^self size`, you will end up with an infinite recursion since `size` is an abstract method. Instead, you should implement the `size` method in the subclasses and override it in the superclass using `subclassResponsibility`.

In summary, the `subclassResponsibility` mechanism in Pharo is a way for superclasses to announce that subclasses should implement certain methods. It allows for flexibility and customization in the subclasses while ensuring that they fulfill their responsibilities.",388,,,,,0.25,0.6243379808840747,3,"['This indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method .', 'Instead of lookup starting in the class of the receiver , it starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the super send occurs .', 'Abstract methods are implemented by convention with the body self subclassResponsibility .']","['By using ` subclassResponsibility ` , the subclasses have the responsibility to define their concrete versions of the method .', 'This is because method lookup follows the inheritance chain , and super sends start the method lookup in the superclass of the class in which the super send is written .', 'Abstract methods are methods that are intended to be implemented by subclasses , and they are typically declared using the ` subclassResponsibility ` mechanism .']",643,948,538,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

RAG,Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0,"

RAG,"If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?","

Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!
I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?

,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
• open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.

, If your Pharo image gets stuck in an infinite loop, you can break out of it by using the debugging tools in Pharo. When an infinite loop occurs, the Pharo image becomes unresponsive. To interrupt the infinite loop and open a debugger, follow these steps:
1. In your web browser, open the Object Inspector by clicking on the Object Inspector link or using the F12 key.
2. In the Object Inspector, open an inspector on the object causing the infinite loop (for example, a counter object).
3. Type `self halt` in the methods list and click the ""do it"" button.
4. Switch back to the Pharo image. You should see a pre-debugger window showing the execution of the halted method.
5. In the pre-debugger window, you can examine the execution or go into the debugger by selecting either ""Save and quit"" or ""Quit"" in the menu.

6. Pharo will ask if you want to save the image before quitting. If you do, the image will be saved with the same state as when you halted the infinite loop, allowing you to pick up where you left off.

Additionally, you can use the keyboard shortcut `CMD-.` (a full stop or a period, depending on where you learned English) to bring up an emergency debugger at any time. To conditionally halt only in specific contexts, you can use the `haltIf:` message instead of `halt`. For instance, you can set a breakpoint in Morph>>openInWorld with `haltIf: #testOpenInWorld`. This way, the image will not freeze when you try to open a new Morph, and the debugger will only open when `testOpenInWorld` is the context.

When you save and quit the Pharo image, it saves a snapshot of all the objects in your memory. This snapshot ensures that nothing is lost on exit. When you start Pharo for the first time, it loads the image file that you specified, containing a snapshot of a large number of objects, including pre-existing code and programming tools. As you work with Pharo, you will send messages to these objects and create new objects. When you quit Pharo, you will normally save a snapshot of all your objects.

(References: Pharo By Example, pages 140, 15, 264-265, 311-312)",360,,,,,0.4444444444444444,0.7175463585484535,8,"['You can also use Halt once or Halt if : aCondition .', ""( that 's a full stop or a period , depending on where you learned English ) ."", 'set the following breakpoint : Morph>>openInWorld "" Add this morph to the world""', 'You should see a pre - debugger window ( Figure 13 - 6 ) showing a WACounter object executing a halt .', 'When you quit Pharo , you will normally save a snapshot that contains all of your objects .', 'As you work with Pharo , you will send messages to these objects , you will create new objects , and some of these objects will die and their memory will be reclaimed ( garbage - collected ) .', 'When you start Pharo for the first time , the Pharo virtual machine loads the image file that you specified .', 'In any case , Pharo will display a prompt to ask you about saving your image .']","['To conditionally halt only in specific contexts , you can use the ` haltIf : ` message instead of ` halt ` .', '( a full stop or a period , depending on where you learned English ) to bring up an emergency debugger at any time .', 'For instance , you can set a breakpoint in Morph>>openInWorld with ` haltIf : # testOpenInWorld ` .', 'You should see a pre - debugger window showing the execution of the halted method .', 'When you quit Pharo , you will normally save a snapshot of all your objects .', 'As you work with Pharo , you will send messages to these objects and create new objects .', 'When you start Pharo for the first time , it loads the image file that you specified , containing a snapshot of a large number of objects , including pre - existing code and programming tools .', 'When you save and quit the Pharo image , it saves a snapshot of all the objects in your memory .', 'Pharo will ask if you want to save the image before quitting .']",220,1389,482,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

column1: , Creating a class in Pharo Smalltalk involves editing the class creation template in the browser. Replace ""Object"" with the name of the existing class you wish to subclass and give the new class a name. You can add instance variables using the message `instanceVariableNames:`. After accepting changes, the classes may still display a ""!"" symbol in the left part. This symbol indicates that the class has not been initialized, and you should add initialization code on the class side.

A new class is usually created by sending the message `subclass:` to an existing class. Pharo supports inheritance through this mechanism, and the root of the inheritance hierarchy is the class Object. However, most applications inherit from Object or its subclasses.

Instance variables are private to a class, and methods are defined in the browser or the debugger. Pharo uses lazy initialization for instance variables, meaning they are initialized when needed, not when the class is created.

To answer the first question, the exclamation mark symbol appearing in the class after accepting changes indicates that the class has not been initialized. You can initialize a class by defining initialization methods on the class side.

To answer the second question, it is generally recommended to put instance creation methods on the class side and utility methods on the instance side. For example, you can define a method `new` on the class side to create and initialize a new instance. In the given example, methods like `colorRed`, `colorGreen`, and `colorBlue` could be defined on the instance side.

, In Pharo or Squeak, you can generate getter/setter code automatically for a class by writing Smalltalk code that dynamically adds accessors for the instance variables. This technique is called ""DynamicAccessors.""

To create DynamicAccessors, you can define a class with a method that traps messages not understood by the class. If the message name matches an instance variable, the class generates and compiles an accessor method on the fly.
Here's a simplified example of how to implement DynamicAccessors:
1. First, create a new class named `DynamicAccessors` with an initialization method `new`.
2. Define the method `doesNotUnderstand:` that traps messages not understood by the class.
3. In the `doesNotUnderstand:` method, check if the message name matches any instance variable. If it does, compile and send the getter method.
self compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.
Now, when you send a message to an instance of `DynamicAccessors` that corresponds to an instance variable, Pharo will generate and call the getter method for you.
You can also create setter methods by naming the message with a trailing colon (e.g., ""variableName:""). The DynamicAccessors class will generate setter methods accordingly.

This technique is useful when you have a long list of instance variables and want to generate their getters and setters automatically without writing the code manually. However, be aware that this approach can make your code harder to read and maintain, so it's essential to document your methods with good comments.",319,,,,,0.5,0.698961161760335,5,"['Let us look at a simple variant of this technique where we have a class that automatically adds accessors for its instance variables on demand : DynamicAcccessors > > doesNotUnderstand :', 'If an instance variable with the same name as the message sent exists , then we ask our class to compile an accessor for that instance variables and we re - send the message .', '* doesNotUnderstand : can not trap self -sends .', 'messages that involve more than one object and that contain at least one colon character ()', ""( self class instVarNames includes : messageName ) ifTrue : [ self class compile : messageName , String cr , ' ^ ' , messageName .]""]","['""    To create DynamicAccessors , you can define a class with a method that traps messages not understood by the class""', 'If the message name matches an instance variable , the class generates and compiles an accessor method on the fly .', 'the method ` doesNotUnderstand : ` that traps messages not understood by the class', 'This technique is called "" DynamicAccessors . ""    To create DynamicAccessors , you can define a class with a method that traps messages not understood by the class .', 'naming the message with a trailing colon ()', 'a simplified example of how to implement DynamicAccessors :', ""aMessage selector asString . , To print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk using Pharo, you can make use of the `Delay` message and a loop. However, the provided code in the question is not printing each message after a delay of 2 seconds as intended. Instead, it prints all messages at once.
One way to achieve the desired behavior is by using a `Delay` message inside a `fork` block, which allows the execution of multiple tasks concurrently. Here's the corrected code:

This code starts by initializing a counter `i` to 0 and sets a condition that the loop should run as long as `i` is less than or equal to 5. Inside the loop, a new delay of 2 seconds is created using `Delay forSeconds:`. The `wait` message is then sent to the delay object to pause the execution for 2 seconds. After waiting, the message '2 seconds are up' is printed to the Transcript using `Transcript show:`. The counter `i` is then incremented, and the loop continues. By using the `fork` keyword, the execution of the inner block is run concurrently with the outer loop, allowing the delay to occur before the next iteration of the loop.
In case you prefer using streams for concatenation instead of the comma operator, you can use the `String streamContents:` message as shown below:

In this example, a `WriteStream` is created, and the counter `i` is used as the condition for the loop. The message '2 seconds are up' is added to the stream along with a carriage return character (`cr`). The loop continues, and the final result is printed to the Transcript using the `Transcript show:` message.",367,,,,,0.0,0.5314258413237387,0,[],[],254,1345,472,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

RAG,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

, Smalltalk, including Pharo, is called dynamically typed because it does not have explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns (Pharo By Example, p. 1). Instead, the type of an object is determined by the class of the object at runtime (pp. 100-101). This is different from statically typed languages, where type checking is done at compile time.

In Pharo, there are only dynamic message sends. For example, instead of providing static class operations, we simply send messages to classes (pp. 100, 16-13). This means that each object can respond to a message in its own way, leading to dynamic, runtime polymorphism.

Variable declarations do not involve message sends. They only allocate space for an object reference, and assignments only bind a variable name to an object within its scope of definition (pp. 100-101). Returns simply pass the computed result back to the sender when a message is received.

Since Pharo's message passing model allows for dynamic dispatching of methods, inheritance, overriding, and super-sending work for class methods, while they don't in statically typed languages like Java (p. 183-184).
Furthermore, each class has its own private class instance variables, and a subclass will inherit those class instance variables, but it will have its own private copies of those variables (p. 92).

Pharo's message passing rules make the code easy to read, with unary messages sent first, followed by binary messages, and finally keyword messages, unless parentheses are used to change the order of execution (p. 71).",243,,,,,0.6363636363636364,0.7280326071094773,7,"['A subclass will inherit those class instance variables , but the subclass will have its own private copies of those variables .', 'For example , in100 6.8 Method lookup follows the inheritance chain stead of providing static class operations , we simply send messages to classes ( which are simply objects ) .', 'Since different objects may have different methods for responding to the same message , the method must be chosen dynamically , when the message is received .', ""This means that inheritance , overriding and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo , whereas they do n't work for static methods in Java ."", 'In Pharo there are only dynamic message sends .', 'Pharo distinguishes such three types of messages to minimize the number of parentheses .', 'A return simply causes the computed result to be returned to the sender .']","['Furthermore , each class has its own private class instance variables , and a subclass will inherit those class instance variables , but it will have its own private copies of those variables ( p. 92 ) .', 'For example , instead of providing static class operations , we simply send messages to classes ( pp . 100 , 16 - 13 ) .', 'This means that each object can respond to a message in its own way , leading to dynamic , runtime polymorphism .', ""Since Pharo 's message passing model allows for dynamic dispatching of methods , inheritance , overriding , and super - sending work for class methods , while they do n't in statically typed languages like Java ( p. 183 - 184 ) ."", 'In Pharo , there are only dynamic message sends .', ""Pharo 's message passing rules make the code easy to read , with unary messages sent first , followed by binary messages , and finally keyword messages , unless parentheses are used to change the order of execution ( p. 71 ) ."", 'Returns simply pass the computed result back to the sender when a message is received .']",224,1377,327,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.
Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.
Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them ""pool variables"", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a ""class variable"" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
, In Pharo, the terms ""instance variable names"" and ""class variable names"" refer to different types of variables with distinct properties and access scopes.

Instance variables are private to the individual instance of a class. Each instance has its own set of instance variables, and they can only be accessed by the instance itself or by the methods defined in its subclasses. Instance variables cannot be directly accessed by other instances of the same class or by the class itself. This is in contrast to Java and C++ where instance variables can be accessed by any other instance of the same class.

Class variables, on the other hand, are shared amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself. Class variables are used to store data that needs to be shared amongst all instances and the class. A subclass will inherit the class variables, but each subclass will have its own private copies of those variables.
Instances of a class cannot directly access the class variables of their class or any other instance's class variables. Instead, they are accessed using class methods.
Here's an example to illustrate the difference:
Transcript show: 'obj1 instance variable value: ', obj1 instanceVariableAt: 1 print.
Transcript show: 'obj1 class variable value: ', obj1 class sharedVariable print.
Transcript show: 'obj2 sharedVariable: ', obj2 sharedVariable print.
Transcript show: 'obj1 sharedVariable: ', obj1 sharedVariable print.
obj1 instanceVariableAt: 1 ifNotNil: [ :x | Transcript show: 'obj1 instance variable overwritten: ', x print ].
Transcript show: 'obj2 sharedVariable: ', obj2 sharedVariable print.

In this example, `MyClass` has one instance variable (not shown), one class variable `sharedVariable`, and is defined in the package `MyPackage`. The class method `initialize` sets the initial value of the class variable `sharedVariable` to 0. The example method `example` creates two instances of `MyClass` and demonstrates accessing both instance variables and class variables.
When you run the example, you'll see output similar to:

As you can see, each instance has its own instance variable value, but all instances and the class share the class variable value.",442,,,,,0.6153846153846154,0.7424936992306884,8,"[""In Pharo , two RAG,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.

, In Smalltalk, methods return the receiver (represented by the ""self"" pseudo-variable) by default if no explicit return statement is defined. This design choice encourages objects to have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing huge, procedural methods (Pelrine, 100-103).

The reason for this is that each object may have its unique implementation for responding to a specific message. For instance, the SmallInteger 3 and the Point (1@2) have their individual methods for handling the message '+ 4'. Therefore, Pharo's message-sending model encourages a style where objects tend to have small methods (Pelrine, 101).

Moreover, this design choice ensures that when a class reimplements a superclass method, the receiver is still returned, as in the case of WebServer and its subclass ReliableWebServer. Using 'self' guarantees that the correct object is returned as the receiver (pp. 95-96).

An example of this can be seen in the Morph class hierarchy, where both Morph and EllipseMorph implement the defaultColor method. The method defined in EllipseMorph overrides the one in Morph, resulting in an ellipse having a yellow default color instead of the blue one for a morph (p. 104).

Additionally, this design choice allows for method chaining, as the result of a method call can be directly used as the receiver for the next method call. For instance, given a MyClass instance 'myInstance', 'myInstance myMethod1' can be followed by 'myInstance myMethod2', making the code more concise and readable.

In summary, methods in Smalltalk (Pharo) return the receiver by default to encourage objects to have small methods, delegate tasks, and follow the message-sending model (Pelrine, 101). This design choice provides a uniform and simple programming model in which everything is an object and objects communicate only by sending each other messages.",286,,,,,0.6363636363636364,0.7185486678067158,7,"['Important By default ( if not specified differently ) a method returns the message receiver .', ""One of the consequences of Pharo 's model of message sending is that it encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility ."", 'We say that EllipseMorph overrides the defaultColor method that it inherits from Morph .', 'It is simple and uniform : everything is an object , and objects communicate only by sending each other messages .', 'Since different objects may have different methods for responding to the same message , the method must be chosen dynamically , when the message is received .', 'We do not tell the SmallInteger 3 or the Point ( 1@2 ) how to respond to the message + 4 .', 'Overriding and extension If we look again at the EllipseMorph class hierarchy in Figure 6 - RAG,Message Not Understood for existing method,"

Ok, so it's like this:
Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?

,

You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

, The question pertains to receiving a ""Message Not Understood"" error for an existing method in Pharo Smalltalk. This error occurs when the method being searched for is not found in the object or any of its superclasses, and the default behavior is to trigger the debugger. However, developers can intercept and handle this error by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in their subclasses.

According to the provided text from the ""Pharo By Example"" textbook (pp. 107-108, 312-313), when a message is not understood by an object, it will start looking for the method `doesNotUnderstand:` in its class hierarchy. If it is found, the debugger will be launched. However, if this method is overridden in any subclass of Object, the object can intercept the error and take alternative actions like forwarding the message to the original target, sending errors, or loading missing methods dynamically.

Therefore, to fix the issue, you can override the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in the receiver class and provide an alternative action to handle the error instead of triggering the debugger. For example, you could provide a default implementation that returns an error message or sends a different message to the receiver.
Here's a simple example of overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to print a message instead of triggering the debugger:
MyClass class >> new: ^ self new initialize.

In this example, we create a new class `MyClass` that overrides the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to print a message instead of triggering the debugger. When we send the non-existent message `#nonExistentMethod` to an instance of this class, it will print the message ""Unknown message: nonExistentMethod"" instead of triggering the debugger.

Keep in mind that this technique should be used carefully, as it can make it harder to find and debug errors in your code. It's generally recommended to provide clear error messages and let the debugger handle unexpected errors in most cases.",359,,,,,0.3076923076923077,0.6508277015498669,4,"['One could easily override the method Object>>doesNotUnderstand : in any subclass of Object and provide a different way of handling the error .', 'If still no method is found for that message , the object will send itself 312 14.6 Intercepting messages not understood the message doesNotUnderstand : with the message selector as its argument .', 'It may be useful to override doesNotUnderstand : to provide some other behaviour .', 'Important It is a good practice that an initialize method start by sending super initialize .']","['However , developers can intercept and handle this error by overriding the ` doesNotUnderstand : ` method in their subclasses .', 'However , if this method is overridden in any subclass of Object , the object can intercept the error and take alternative actions like forwarding the message to the original target , sending errors , or loading missing methods dynamically .', '107 - 108 , 312 - 313 ) , when a message is not understood by an object , it will start looking for the method ` doesNotUnderstand : ` in its class hierarchy .', 'Therefore , to fix the issue , you can override the ` doesNotUnderstand : ` method in the receiver class and provide an alternative action to handle the error instead of triggering the debugger .', 'MyClass class > > initialize       super class > > initialize .']",188,1404,498,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

, To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you cannot directly get an OrderedCollection of the keys with the values in descending order using a single message. However, you can achieve this by converting the Dictionary to an Array of associations first, then creating an OrderedCollection from that.

First, you need to create an Array of associations from the Dictionary using the message `asArray`. Then, send the `asSortedCollection` message to the Array to sort it based on the values in descending order. Lastly, you can extract the keys from the sorted OrderedCollection to get the desired OrderedCollection of keys in descending order based on their corresponding values.
aDictionary := Dictionary newFrom: { #blue -> Color blue. #yellow -> Color yellow }.
sortedOrderedCollection := sortedAssociations asSortedCollection: [ :assoc1 :assoc2 | assoc1 value asNumber > assoc2 value asNumber ].
sortedOrderedCollection do: [ :assoc | keys add: assoc key ].

The `sortedOrderedCollection` will contain the `OrderedCollection` of associations (key-value pairs) sorted based on their values in descending order. The last step extracts the keys from this collection and stores them in a new `OrderedCollection` named `keys`.",196,,,,,0.2857142857142857,0.6413948022848797,2,"['We can create a Dictionary from a collection of associations , or we may convert a dictionary to an array of associations .', '* To sort a collection , send it the message asSortedCollection .']","['First , you need to create an Array of associations from the Dictionary using the message ` asArray ` .', 'However , you can achieve this by converting the Dictionary to an Array of associations first , then creating an OrderedCollection from that .', 'Then , send the ` asSortedCollection ` message to the Array to sort it based on the values in descending order .']",212,1385,296,0,[],pharo,mistral:instruct

,

If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.
If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.
Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.
If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
, To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can follow these steps:
1. Ensure that both arrays have the same number of elements.
2. Iterate through each element of the first array and check if it exists in the second array. If it does, continue iterating. If not, then the arrays do not contain the same elements.
Here is an example implementation in Pharo Smalltalk:
arr1 size = arr2 size ifFalse: [ ^ false ].
arr1 do: [ :a | (arr2 includes: a) ifFalse: [ ^ false ]. ].
The `includes:` message is used to check if an array contains a specific element.

However, it is essential to note that both arrays must have the same objects as elements, not just objects with the same values. If the arrays contain different objects with equal values, they will not be considered equal even if they contain the same elements. In that case, you will need to override the `hash` method for the objects to make them comparable in sets or dictionaries.

RAG+CoI,Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?,"

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.

, Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed programming language inspired by Smalltalk. It is highly portable and can run on various operating systems including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi. Pharo is the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework. Pharo's ecosystem consists of six fundamental elements: a dynamically-typed language with a simple syntax, a live coding environment, a powerful IDE, a rich library, a culture of changes and improvements, and a welcoming community. Pharo is unique in that it offers a live coding environment, allowing programmers to modify code while the code executes without interrupting their workflow. Pharo's community values and encourages changes and improvements, and offers a stable platform for professional software development and research into dynamic languages and environments. To get started, you can download the Seaside One-Click Experience, which includes a prepackaged version of Pharo with many Seaside-related packages preloaded, as well as a helpful Seaside control panel. From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:
Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.

Another method is `copyReplaceAll:`:`with:` to create a new String with some substrings replaced. It's important to note that the arguments are substrings rather than individual characters.
You can also modify a String by concatenating it with other strings using the `,` operator or the `,:` message:

Strings are also comparable, so you can use methods like `<`, `>`, `=`, etc. to compare them. Additionally, you can use the `asSortedCollection:` message to sort a collection of strings or any other comparable objects.

, In Pharo, there are several methods to query the class of an object. These methods include `class`, `isMemberOf:`, `isKindOf:`, and `respondsTo:`.
`class` is a message that returns the class of an object. For instance, `1 class` returns `SmallInteger`.
`isMemberOf:` checks if an object is an instance of a specific class. For example, `1 isMemberOf: SmallInteger` returns `true`.
`isKindOf:` returns `true` if the receiver's class is either the same as or a subclass of the argument class. For instance, `1 isKindOf: Object` and `1 isKindOf: Number` both return `true`.
`respondsTo:` checks if an object understands a given message selector. For instance, `1 respondsTo: #+` returns `true`.
Additionally, there are methods to query all instances of a class, such as `Object new class allInstances` and `ByteString allInstances`, which can be useful for debugging and code discovery.

It is important to note that classes are objects too, and every object is an instance of a class. Classes define the structure and behavior of their instances via instance variables and methods. Each class has a unique metaclass that defines its structure and behavior.

Overall, there are various methods to query the class of an object in Pharo, which can be useful for development tools and debugging. However, it is important to be aware of the implications of the uniformity of the object model and the reflective nature of the system.",220,,,,,0.5,0.7239210931232869,5,"['You can ask any object about its class using the message class .', 'Each class is the unique instance of its metaclass .', 'Conversely , you can ask if an object is an instance of a specific class : 1 isMemberOf :', 'Classes define the structure of their instances via private instance variables and the behaviour of their instances via public methods .', 'Since classes are objects , and every object is an instance of a class , it follows that classes must also be instances of classes .']","['In Pharo , there are several methods to query the class of an object .', '` class ` is a message that returns the class of an object .', 'Each class has a unique metaclass that defines its structure and behavior .', 'Classes define the structure and behavior of their instances via instance variables and methods .', 'It is important to note that classes are objects too , and every object is an instance of a class .', 'For instance , ` 1 class ` returns ` SmallInteger ` .    ` isMemberOf : ` checks if an object is an instance of a specific class .']",187,4469,325,5,"['What methods allow querying the class of an object in Pharo?', 'What function does Object>>isKindOf perform?', 'What are instances of classes?', 'What kind of objects are classes?', 'What is ""Every object"" an instance of?']",pharo,mistral:instruct
