GenAI,"What does the ""yield"" keyword do in Python?","

What functionality does the yield keyword in Python provide?
For example, I'm trying to understand this code1:
What happens when the method _get_child_candidates is called?
Is a list returned? A single element? Is it called again? When will subsequent calls stop?
1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. , The `yield` keyword in Python is used to define a generator function. A generator function is a special kind of function that can be iterated over, producing one value at a time, instead of computing and returning a complete list or tuple at once.
In the provided code snippet, the function `_get_child_candidates` is defined as a generator. It uses the `yield` keyword to return each child node as an individual value.

When `_get_child_candidates` is called, it does not return a list or a single element. Instead, it generates a sequence of values, which are the child nodes that meet the specified conditions. Each time a value is requested from the generator, the next yield expression is executed, returning the next value.

The caller function in the provided code example iterates through the generator by popping an element (a node) and assigning it to the variable `node`. Then it calculates the distance between this node and the object in question. If the distance is within the specified range, the values from this node are extended to the `result` list. Afterwards, the generator is called again to get the child candidates of the current node, which will be the next value returned by the generator.

The subsequent calls to the generator will stop when there are no more child nodes left to yield from the generator function. In the provided example, when `candidates` is an empty list, the generator will no longer yield any values.

In summary, when `_get_child_candidates` is called, a generator is returned that produces a sequence of child nodes that meet certain conditions. The caller function then iterates over this generator, taking each child node as it is generated. The generator will stop producing values when there are no more child nodes left.",290,,,,,0.0,0.5006773767447747,0,[],[],394,1198,352,0,[],python,mistral:instruct

However, if the string contains non-numeric characters, Python will raise a `ValueError`. For instance:
print(e)  # Output: invalid literal for int(): Hello
When converting a string to a float (type float), you can use the `float()` function. It converts the string to a floating-point number. For example:
float_number = float(value)  # float conversion is successful
It's important to note that Python's int function does not perform rounding when converting a float to an integer. It only keeps the whole number part and discards the fractional part.

column1: It's boilerplate code that protects users from accidentally invoking the script when they didn't intend to. Here are some common problems when the guard is omitted from a script:

• If you import the guardless script in another script (e.g. import my_script_without_a_name_eq_main_guard), then the latter script will trigger the former to run at import time and using the second script's command line arguments. This is almost always a mistake.

• If you have a custom class in the guardless script and save it to a pickle file, then unpickling it in another script will trigger an import of the guardless script, with the same problems outlined in the previous bullet.
To better understand why and how this matters, we need to take a step back to understand how Python initializes scripts and how this interacts with its module import mechanism.
Whenever the Python interpreter reads a source file, it does two things:
• it sets a few special variables like __name__, and then
• it executes all of the code found in the file.

, The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is used in Python programs to prevent the code inside it from being executed when the module is imported as a library, but only when the file is run as a standalone script. This idiom is important because when a Python file is imported as a module, the code inside `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` is skipped, and the functions and classes defined in the module become available to the importing script. If this code was not skipped, it would cause unwanted side effects or errors when the module is imported.
For example, consider the following simple Python script:

Now, if you run this script as a standalone script, it will print ""Hello from mymodule!"" followed by ""Hello, world!"". However, if you import this module as a library in another script, it will only define the `hello()` function without executing the `print` statement, as shown below:
By using the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement, we can ensure that the code inside it is only executed when the script is run as a standalone script and not when it is imported as a library.

As a side note, it is essential to understand the difference between running a Python file as a script and importing it as a library. When you run a Python file as a script, Python sets the special `__name__` variable to `""__main__""` by default. However, when you import a Python file as a library, Python sets the `__name__` variable to the name of the file (without the extension). Therefore, using `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` allows us to differentiate between these two use cases and write code accordingly.",295,,,,,0.25,0.603376945907611,2,"[""If the program is running as a script , _ _ name _ _ has the value ' _ _"", 'Otherwise , if the module is being imported , the test code is skipped .']","['When you run a Python file as a script , Python sets the special ` _ _ name _ _', 'If this code was not skipped , it would cause unwanted side effects or errors when the module is imported .']",302,1288,418,0,[],python,mistral:instruct

Explanation: the methods based on + (including the implied use in sum) are, of necessity, O(L**2) when there are L sublists -- as the intermediate result list keeps getting longer, at each step a new intermediate result list object gets allocated, and all the items in the previous intermediate result must be copied over (as well as a few new ones added at the end). So, for simplicity and without actual loss of generality, say you have L sublists of M items each: the first M items are copied back and forth L-1 times, the second M items L-2 times, and so on
column2: total number of copies is M times the sum of x for x from 1 to L excluded, i.e., M * (L**2)/2.

RAG,How to use a global variable in a function?,"

How do I create or use a global variable inside a function?
How do I use a global variable that was defined in one function inside other functions?

Failing to use the global keyword where appropriate often causes UnboundLocalError. The precise rules for this are explained at UnboundLocalError on local variable when reassigned after first use. Generally, please close other questions as a duplicate of that question when an explanation is sought, and this question when someone simply needs to know the global keyword.

,

You can use a global variable within other functions by declaring it as global within each function that assigns a value to it:
global globvar    # Needed to modify global copy of globvar
print(globvar)     # No need for global declaration to read value of globvar

Since it's unclear whether globvar = 1 is creating a local variable or changing a global variable, Python defaults to creating a local variable, and makes you explicitly choose the other behavior with the global keyword.
See other answers if you want to share a global variable across modules.

, To use or create a global variable inside a function, you need to declare it as global before using it. This tells the interpreter that you mean the global variable with the same name, instead of creating a new local variable. Global variables are accessible from any function and persist from one function call to the next. They are often used as flags or indicators of conditions.
For instance, consider the following example where we have a global variable `verbose` that controls the level of detail in the output:

If you try to reassign a global variable without declaring it as global, you might get an `UnboundLocalError`. In this case, if you want to reassign the global variable `been_called` inside the function `example2()`:
Similarly, for a mutable value like a list or dictionary, you can modify its elements without declaring the variable, but if you want to reassign it, you need to declare it as global:

Global variables can be helpful, but they can make programs hard to debug, especially when dealing with larger datasets. Therefore, it is recommended to avoid using too many global variables and instead focus on local variables within functions.

RAG,How do I get a substring of a string in Python?,"

I want to get a new string from the third character to the end of the string, e.g. myString[2:end]. If omitting the second part means 'to the end', and if you omit the first part, does it start from the start?
Python calls this concept ""slicing"" and it works on more than just strings. Take a look here for a comprehensive introduction.

, To get a substring of a string in Python, you can use slicing. The syntax is `string[start:end]`. If you omit the start index, it defaults to the beginning of the string, and if you omit the end index, it goes to the end of the string. For example, `fruit = 'banana'`, `fruit[:3]` returns 'ban', and `fruit[3:]` returns 'ana'. If the first index is greater than or equal to the second, the result is an empty string.

Regarding your question, to get a substring from the third character to the end of the string, you can write it as `myString[2:]`. If you omit the second index, it defaults to the end of the string. Therefore, `myString[2:]` and `myString[2:end]` are equivalent.
For instance, given the string `myString = 'Monty Python'`, `myString[2:]` returns `'ony Python'`.

Keep in mind that strings in Python are immutable, meaning you cannot change the value of an existing string by using the indexing method. However, you can create a new string by using the slicing method.

For more information, you can refer to pages 71 to 74 in the ""Think Python"" textbook.",181,,,,,0.2857142857142857,0.6107239570401378,2,"['If you omit the first index ( before the colon ) , the slice starts at the beginning of the string .', 'If you omit the second , the slice goes to the end .']","['If you omit the start index , it defaults to the beginning of the string , and if you omit the end index , it goes to the end of the string .', 'If you omit the second index , it defaults to the end of the string .']",148,1450,267,0,[],python,mistral:instruct

First, let's discuss deleting files. The `os.remove()` function deletes a file if it exists. For example, to delete a file named 'example.txt', you can use the following code:

What functionality does the yield keyword in Python provide?
For example, I'm trying to understand this code1:
What happens when the method _get_child_candidates is called?

1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "

• The loop iterates on a list, but the list expands while the loop is being iterated. It's a concise way to go through all these nested data even if it's a bit dangerous since you can end up with an infinite loop. In this case, candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) exhausts all the values of the generator, but while keeps creating new generator objects which will produce different values from the previous ones since it's not applied on the same node.

Usually, we pass a list to it:
But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:

And it works because Python does not care if the argument of a method is a list or not. Python expects iterables so it will work with strings, lists, tuples, and generators! This is called duck typing and is one of the reasons why Python is so cool. But this is another story, for another question...

Iteration is a process implying iterables (implementing the __iter__() method) and iterators (implementing the __next__() method).
Iterables are any objects you can get an iterator from. Iterators are objects that let you iterate on iterables.

, The ""yield"" keyword in Python is used to define a generator, which is a special type of iterable object in Python. It allows a function to act like an iterator, producing values one at a time as they are requested (Think Python, p. 125). In the provided code snippet, the method ""_get_child_candidates"" is defined as a generator function since it uses the ""yield"" keyword.
When the method is called, it does not return a list or a single element

, The most Pythonic way to find an item in a list in Python depends on the specific use case and the size of the list. In general, using the `in` keyword is a simple and commonly used approach for checking if an item exists in a list. For small lists, this approach is sufficient and efficient. However, for larger lists, methods such as binary search or bisection search can offer better performance.

The `in` operator in Python uses a linear search algorithm for lists, which checks each element in the list one by one until it finds the target item. As the list grows larger, the search time increases linearly. On the other hand, binary search and bisection search use a logarithmic time complexity algorithm, which divides the search space in half and checks only the relevant half at each step.

The `in` operator can also be used with dictionaries. In this case, the algorithm used depends on the underlying data structure, with Python dictionaries utilizing a hashtable data structure that allows for constant time complexity for the `in` operator.

It's important to note that a reverse lookup, i.e., finding a key given its corresponding value, is generally slower than a forward lookup. This is because a reverse lookup involves searching through the entire data structure.

In summary, for small lists, using the `in` operator is an efficient and Pythonic way to find an item. For larger lists, methods such as binary search or bisection search can offer better performance.

, The append method and extend method are two ways to modify lists in Python, but they have different functionalities. (p. 91-92)

The append method adds a single element to the end of a list. For example, given a list t1 = [1, 2], appending the number 3 to it results in the list [1, 2, 3]. The return value from append is None. (p. 92)

On the other hand, the extend method takes a list as an argument and appends all of its elements to the end of the original list. For instance, if we have lists t1 = [1, 2] and t2 = [3, 4], extending t1 with t2 results in the list [1, 2, 3, 4]. In this case, t2 remains unmodified. (pp. 92)
While both methods modify lists, the key difference is in the number of elements that are being added. append() adds a single element, whereas extend() adds multiple elements as a list. (p. 97-98)
Here's a summary of the differences:
- Adds a single element to the end of a list.
- Adds all the elements of another list to the end of the current list.
- The original list and the argument list remain unchanged unless explicitly modified.

In conclusion, choose the appropriate method based on your requirements: use append() when you need to add a single element, and extend() when you need to add multiple elements as a list. The `count()` method returns the number of occurrences of a specified substring within a given string. For example, `'a'.count('banana')` returns `1`.

Python provides several ways to execute programs or call system commands, including the use of pipes represented by pipe objects.
To launch an external command like 'ls' in Unix using Python, you can use the os.popen() function with the command as an argument. For instance, you can write:

The return value of the os.popen() function is an object that behaves like an open file, allowing you to read the output of the command one line at a time using the readline() method or read the entire output at once using the read() method. When you call a function, the flow of execution in the program changes. Functions can call other functions, leading to a hierarchical structure of function calls. This can help you modularize your code and create reusable components.

• If you import the guardless script in another script (e.g. import my_script_without_a_name_eq_main_guard), then the latter script will trigger the former to run at import time and using the second script's command line arguments. This is almost always a mistake.

, The `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` idiom is used in Python programs to ensure that the code within the block runs only when the file is run as a standalone script, and not when the file is imported as a module.

When a Python file is executed directly, the special `__name__` variable is set to `""__main__""`. However, when a file is imported as a module, `__name__` takes the name of the imported module. By using this idiom, the code inside the block can be used to perform tasks that are specific to the script, such as running tests or displaying output, without interfering with the functionality of the module when it is imported.
For example, consider the following `wc.py` script:
""""""Count the number of lines in a file""""""

When `wc.py` is run as a script, it will print the number of lines in the file `wc.py`. However, when `wc.py` is imported as a module, the code inside the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` block is skipped, and only the `linecount` function is defined and made available for use.

It's important to include this idiom in scripts that are intended to be run as standalone programs, but that also define functions or classes that may be used as modules in other programs. This ensures that the code within the script does not interfere with the intended use of the functions or classes when they are imported.

The warning in the text suggests that if you are trying to import a module that has already been imported or run, you may need to restart the Python interpreter or use the `reload` function to reload the module. This is because Python caches the compiled bytecode of imported modules, so re-importing the same module does not cause it to be re-executed.

This idiom is used frequently in Python programming, and is important for ensuring that code runs correctly in different contexts. It's a best practice to include this idiom in all of your Python scripts that are intended to be run as standalone programs, as well as in modules that may be imported by other programs. (Think Python, pages 144-145)
print(""The square root of {0} * {1} is {2}"".format(x, y, math.sqrt(x * y)))

When `example.py` is run as a script, it will print the square root of `5 * 3`. However, when `example.py` is imported as a module, the code inside the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` block is skipped, and the `math.sqrt` function is made available for use in the imported module.",463,,,,,0.1875,0.6137099750625592,3,"['What is the value of _ _ name _ _ when the module is being imported ?', ""For example , suppose you have a file named wc.py with the following code : def linecount(filename ): count = 0 for line in open(filename ): count + = 1 return count print(linecount('wc.py ' ) )"", '* If you are using import to read a module , remember that you have to restart the interpreter or use reload to read a modified file .']","['However , when a file is imported as a module , ` _ _ name _ _ ` takes the name of the imported module .', 'For example , consider the following ` wc.py ` script :    ` ` ` python   def linecount(filename ):       "" "" "" Count the number of lines in a file "" "" ""       with open(filename ) as f :           lines = f.readlines ( )       return len(lines )    if _ _ name _ _', 'The warning in the text suggests that if you are trying to import a module that has already been imported or run , you may need to restart the Python interpreter or use the ` reload ` function to reload the module .']",302,2612,627,4,"['What is the value of __name__ when wc is being run as a script?', ""What should you use Python's script mode for?"", 'What does the Python interpreter do when in script mode?', 'Why is it important to ensure that the program Python is trying to run is the one you are editing?']",python,mistral:instruct

Explanation: the methods based on + (including the implied use in sum) are, of necessity, O(L**2) when there are L sublists -- as the intermediate result list keeps getting longer, at each step a new intermediate result list object gets allocated, and all the items in the previous intermediate result must be copied over (as well as a few new ones added at the end). So, for simplicity and without actual loss of generality, say you have L sublists of M items each: the first M items are copied back and forth L-1 times, the second M items L-2 times, and so on
column2: total number of copies is M times the sum of x for x from 1 to L excluded, i.e., M * (L**2)/2.

column1: The list comprehension just generates one list, once, and copies each item over (from its original place of residence to the result list) also exactly once.

How do I create or use a global variable inside a function?
How do I use a global variable that was defined in one function inside other functions?

Failing to use the global keyword where appropriate often causes UnboundLocalError. The precise rules for this are explained at UnboundLocalError on local variable when reassigned after first use. Generally, please close other questions as a duplicate of that question when an explanation is sought, and this question when someone simply needs to know the global keyword.

,

You can use a global variable within other functions by declaring it as global within each function that assigns a value to it:
global globvar    # Needed to modify global copy of globvar
print(globvar)     # No need for global declaration to read value of globvar

Since it's unclear whether globvar = 1 is creating a local variable or changing a global variable, Python defaults to creating a local variable, and makes you explicitly choose the other behavior with the global keyword.
See other answers if you want to share a global variable across modules.

, Global variables in programming are variables that can be accessed from any function, whereas local variables only exist within the function in which they are defined. Global variables persist from one function call to the next. It is common to use global variables for flags, which are boolean variables that indicate whether a certain condition is true. For instance, a program might use a global variable named `verbose` to control the level of detail in the output.

However, when you try to reassign a global variable inside a function, you might be surprised, as Python assumes that the variable is local and under that assumption, you are reading it before writing it. To reassign a global variable inside a function, you must declare it as global before you use it. This tells the interpreter that in this function, whenever you say ""been_called,"" for example, you mean the global variable, not a local one.

, A string slice in Python can take an optional third index to specify the step size, which is the number of spaces between successive characters in the slice. RAG+CoI,"How do I create a directory, and any missing parent directories?","

How do I create a directory at a given path, and also create any missing parent directories along that path? For example, the Bash command mkdir -p /path/to/nested/directory does this.
For older versions of Python, I see two answers with good qualities, each with a small flaw, so I will give my take on it:
As noted in comments and elsewhere, there's a race condition &ndash
column2: if the directory is created between the os.path.exists and the os.makedirs calls, the os.makedirs will fail with an OSError. Unfortunately, blanket-catching OSError and continuing is not foolproof, as it will ignore a failure to create the directory due to other factors, such as insufficient permissions, full disk, etc.

column1: One option would be to trap the OSError and examine the embedded error code (see Is there a cross-platform way of getting information from Python’s OSError):
Alternatively, there could be a second os.path.exists, but suppose another created the directory after the first check, then removed it before the second one &ndash

Depending on the application, the danger of concurrent operations may be more or less than the danger posed by other factors such as file permissions. The developer would have to know more about the particular application being developed and its expected environment before choosing an implementation.
Modern versions of Python improve this code quite a bit, both by exposing FileExistsError (in 3.3+)...
...and by allowing a keyword argument to os.makedirs called exist_ok (in 3.2+).
<pre><code>os.makedirs(""path/to/directory"", exist_ok=True)  # succeeds even if directory exists.

, To create a directory and any missing parent directories at a given path in Python, you can use the `os` module's `makedirs` function. This function creates the directory specified by the given path, along with any missing parent directories.
The `exist_ok` argument is optional. If set to `True` (default is `False`), the function will not raise an error if the directory already exists.

When using `makedirs`, it's important to note that the function will raise an exception if the directory cannot be created due to a permission issue. In such a case, you should handle the exception using a `try`/`except` statement.
As an alternative to using `makedirs`, you could create the parent directories manually using a loop and the `os.makedirs` call for the final directory.
Here's an example for creating a nested directory using this approach:

column1: It is my understanding (as well as the understanding of the creator of the language) that the intended usage for dict(**y) is for creating dictionaries for readability purposes, e.g.:

Despite what Guido says, dict(x, **y) is in line with the dict specification, which btw. works for both Python 2 and 3. The fact that this only works for string keys is a direct consequence of how keyword parameters work and not a short-coming of dict. Nor is using the ** operator in this place an abuse of the mechanism, in fact, ** was designed precisely to pass dictionaries as keywords.

Again, it doesn't work for 3 when keys are not strings. The implicit calling contract is that namespaces take ordinary dictionaries, while users must only pass keyword arguments that are strings. All other callables enforced it. dict broke this consistency in Python 2:
TypeError: foo() keywords must be strings
This inconsistency was bad given other implementations of Python (PyPy, Jython, IronPython). Thus it was fixed in Python 3, as this usage could be a breaking change.
I submit to you that it is malicious incompetence to intentionally write code that only works in one version of a language or that only works given certain arbitrary constraints.

What functionality does the yield keyword in Python provide?

1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "
<pre><code># Here you create the method of the node object that will return the generator
# Here is the code that will be called each time you use the generator object:
# If there is still a child of the node object on its left
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If there is still a child of the node object on its right
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If the function arrives here, the generator will be considered empty
# There are no more than two values: the left and the right children
<pre><code># Create an empty list and a list with the current object reference
# Loop on candidates (they contain only one element at the beginning)
# Get the last candidate and remove it from the list
# Get the distance between obj and the candidate
# If the distance is ok, then you can fill in the result
# Add the children of the candidate to the candidate's list
# so the loop will keep running until it has looked
# at all the children of the children of the children, etc. of the candidate

• The loop iterates on a list, but the list expands while the loop is being iterated. It's a concise way to go through all these nested data even if it's a bit dangerous since you can end up with an infinite loop. In this case, candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) exhausts all the values of the generator, but while keeps creating new generator objects which will produce different values from the previous ones since it's not applied on the same node.
• The extend() method is a list object method that expects an iterable and adds its values to the list.
Usually, we pass a list to it:
But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:
• You don't need to read the values twice.
• You may have a lot of children and you don't want them all stored in memory.

And it works because Python does not care if the argument of a method is a list or not. Python expects iterables so it will work with strings, lists, tuples, and generators! This is called duck typing and is one of the reasons why Python is so cool. But this is another story, for another question...

, The ""yield"" keyword in Python is used to define a generator function, which is a special type of fruitful function that can return multiple values iteratively. Generator functions are commonly used in situations where you want to produce a sequence of values on the fly, without having to create a list or an array that stores all the values at once.

In the given example, the `_get_child_candidates` method is a generator function that yields the left and right child nodes of a tree node based on the given distance criteria. When this method is called, it does not return a list or a single element. Instead, it generates a sequence of nodes that match the given criteria, which can then be iterated over in the caller function.

The caller function iterates through the generated sequence of nodes until there are no more nodes to process. When `_get_child_candidates` is called, it does not stop executing until it has yielded all the matching child nodes. Subsequent calls to this method will continue from where the previous call left off, generating the next matching child node.

In summary, when you call a generator function in Python, it returns a generator object, which can be iterated over to produce the desired sequence of values. The generator function does not return a list or a single element, and it can be called multiple times to generate more values as needed.
